name: A. M. Burns
write_up: A. M. Burns: employee of the Pacific Mail Steamers who captained the Fremont on the Portland-San Francisco mail route from 1852-53.
notes:
Lewis & Dryden's Marine History of the Pacific Northwest at http://books.google.com/books?id=seRDAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA47&lpg=PA47&dq=captain+of+the+brig+Fremont+1853&source=bl&ots=OxqDZKiCid&sig=6NAWIMA5xS98-aFH0BmD6bUj8cA&hl=en&sa=X&ei=s8SIU-qJDompsASRi4GgDw&ved=0CCkQ6AEwAA#v=snippet&q=%22fremont%22%20%22burns%22&f=false

name: A. P. More
write_up: A.P. More: one of three More brothers who played an important role in developing the Santa Barbara region in the mid-nineteenth century. Between 1859-70, A.P., T. Wallace, and Henry H. More acquired sole proprietorship of Santa Rosa Island, where they developed a thriving sheep-raising venture.

name: Absalom B. Stuart
write_up: Absalom B. Stuart (1830-87): a prosperous doctor born in Pennsylvania. He trained at the Berkshire Medical College at Williams College in Massachusetts and at the Bellevue Medical College in New York. He relocated to Minnesota Territory and California, where he was licensed to practice medicine. In 1879, he appears in the Santa Barbara (CA) voting records; it is likely at this point that he met with George Nidever and Carl Dittman. Stuart was associated with The Sanitarian, a monthly medical journal based in New York.
notes:
http://books.google.com/books?id=XIQCAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA400&lpg=PA400&dq=%22Absalom+B.+Stuart%22+santa+barbara+california&source=bl&ots=Z2VyTu4VIV&sig=ALYHhSoNsFotv0DgbbozDVd2q58&hl=en&sa=X&ei=B3WKU6jEHta1sASOz4GQBw&ved=0CCkQ6AEwAA#v=onepage&q=Absalom%20B.%20Stuart&f=false

http://www.petalumapost.com/pastissues/webpostmarch04.pdf

http://elane.stanford.edu/public/L29628/1885-XXVIII.pdf; records from Ancestry.com (voting, civil war pensions, census records, etc.)

name: Adam Clark Vroman
write_up: Adam Clark Vroman (1856-1916): a photographer. Vroman founded Vroman's Bookstore in Pasadena, California and became well-known for his photographs of California landscapes and architecture before he began documenting the rituals and daily lives of various Native American groups, work conducted in coordination with the Bureau of American Ethnology.

name: Adelaide LeMert Doran
write_up: Adelaide LeMert Doran (1908-87): a teacher and author. Doran attended two campuses of the Universities of California, UC-Los Angeles and UC-Southern California, before working as a history teacher. Her published works include The Ranch that was Robbins': Santa Catalina Island, California (1963) and Pieces of Eight Channel Islands: A Bibliographical Guide and Source Book (1980).

name: Adelbert von Chamisso
write_up: Adelbert von Chamisso (1781-1838): an author and botanist who accompanied the Russian Romanzov Expedition (1815-18), an around-the-world voyage of scientific discovery. Von Chamisso published a diary of the experience, which included collecting in San Francisco Bay. A German-language lyricist (his family had fled France during the Revolution), von Chamisso may be best known today as the author of Peter Schlemihls wundersame Geschichte( Peter Schlemihl’s Miraculous Story)

name: Adele Ogden
write_up: Adele Ogden (1902-90): public school teacher and preeminent researcher of California maritime history. She earned a PhD from the University of California and is author of The California Sea-Otter Trade, 1784-1848.
notes: US Census, 1940.  The Oakland Tribune 26 Aug 1925 and 2 Dec 1941.

name: Adolphus L. Heermann
write_up: Adolphus L. Heermann (1818?-65?): a medical doctor and field ornithologist who was elected to the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia. Heermaan served as part of the US Army Corps of Topographical Engineers in the West, completing surveys that helped to map routes for the Pacific Railroad. Heerman initially crossed the Rocky Mountains with John C. Frémont and then in the 1850s was appointed as surgeon and naturalist to Lieutenant R. S. Williamson's command.

name: Albert B. Elsasser
write_up: Albert B. Elsasser (1918-99): research anthropologist at the Phoebe Hearst Museum of Anthropology at the University of California, Berkeley (formerly the Lowie Museum of Anthropology).  Elsasser was a doctoral student of Robert F. Heizer, with whom he later co-edited the University of California Archaeological Survey No. 55 "Original Accounts of the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island," a compilation of archival and published accounts about the Lone Woman’s life and "rescue."

name: Albert Einstein
write_up: Albert Einstein (1879-1955): a German-Jewish theoretical physicist. Einstein was educated at the Swiss Federal Polytechnic School in Zurich and, before becoming established in the academy, was an assistant in the Swiss Patent Office. Throughout his career he taught at the Universities of Bern, Zürich, Berlin, and Princeton, among other institutions. Einstein's published works (and just a few of his most important scientific contributions) include "The Theory of Relativity" (1905), The Investigations on Theory of Brownian Movement (1926), and The Evolution of Physics (1938). Einstein was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921.

name: Albert Ernest Jenks
write_up: Albert Ernest Jenks (1869-1953): archaeologist and anthropologist who was largely self-taught in these fields. He earned a PhD in economics from the University of Wisconsin, writing about the economic practices of Native peoples of the Great Lakes. Jenks was hired by the Bureau of American Ethnology as assistant chief of the Bureau of Non-Christian Tribes in the Philippines and was then promoted to chief of the Ethnological Survey. Later, Jenks founded the University of Minnesota's Department of Anthropology. He was a member of the National Academy of Science.

name: Albert West
write_up: Albert West: with Thomas Thompson, co-owner of the Oakland-based Thompson & West publishing business.

name: Aleš Ferdinand Hrdlička
write_up: Aleš Ferdinand Hrdlička (1869-1943): an anthropologist. Hrdlička briefly practiced medicine before leaving the United States for Paris to study physical anthropology, a field in which he was employed for the rest of his life. After research and expeditions with the American Museum of Natural History, he served as the assistant curator of the United States National Museum's Division of Physical Anthropology and later took part in expeditions with the Bureau of American Ethnology. Hrdlička was associated with numerous scientific and anthropological associations, including the Anthropological Society of Washington, the International Congress of Americanists, and the National Research Council. He edited the American Journal of Physical Anthropology and published widely in his field.

name: Alexander III of Macedon
write_up: Alexander III of Macedon, "the Great" (356-23 BCE): a Greek king, military leader, politician, and explorer. Alexander inherited his father's kingdom, consolidated power within Greece, and conquered Egypt, Asia Minor, and the Persian Empire in just a decade of military campaigns. Through the uniting of peoples from three continents, Alexander helped facilitate unprecedented cooperation in scholarship, culture, and scientific learning.

name: Alexander Selkirk
write_up: Alexander Selkirk (1676-1721): Scottish sailor who spent four years in total isolation on an uninhabited island in what is now called the Juan Fernández Archipelago. After being marooned by his crew, he became quite resourceful, building huts, hunting feral animals, and fashioning various weapons and other utensils on the island. Upon his rescue and return to land, he became the center of attention in England and his life inspired many fictional works, including Daniel Defoe's Robinson Crusoe.

name: Alexander von Humboldt
write_up: Alexander von Humboldt (1769-1859): German naturalist and explorer, educated at the Universities of Frankfurt and Göttingen. An expedition to Central and South America led to his collection of extensive data on plant life, topography, and climate, which he spent many years preparing for publication. He corresponded extensively with other thinkers of his day, popularizing science. He was influential in the construction of observatories worldwide in search of new geomagnetic data. His most influential published work was Kosmos (five volumes, 1845-62).

name: Alexis Godey
write_up: Alexis Godey (1818?-89): a Missouri-born frontiersman, most likely of French-Canadian parentage. Along with George Nidever, Godey was a member of Benjamin Bonneville's exploration of the Rocky Mountains and took part in Joseph Walker's 1833 expedition to California. He served as a scout for John Charles Frémont and, with "Kit" Carson, was instrumental in the United States' taking of California under Frémont. Later in life, Godey made his living as a rancher.

name: Alfred Bertram "Bud" Guthrie, Jr.
write_up: Alfred Bertram "Bud" Guthrie, Jr. (1901-91): a journalist, author, and screenwriter. Guthrie attended the University of Washington and the University of Montana and published works including The Big Sky (1947), The Way West (1949; 1950 Pulitzer Prize winner), and Fair Land, Fair Land (1982).

name: Alfred Louis Kroeber
write_up: Alfred Louis Kroeber (1876-1960): an anthropologist who played a major role in shaping his academic field during its inception. After receiving Columbia University’s first PhD in anthropology, under the direction of Franz Boas, Kroeber established the Department of Anthropology at the University of California, Berkeley, where he remained for the rest of his career. Among other work, Kroeber conducted extensive research on the languages and cultures of California’s Native people, publishing the Handbook of the Indians of California in 1925. He and his colleagues at Berkeley described California's Mission Indians as having become so acculturated as to have lost their unique identities. This assessment had lasting consequences for academic and popular understandings of California Indians, and for many indigenous groups' later difficulty in gaining status as federally recognized tribes. Kroeber is perhaps best known today for his work with Ishi, a "wild Indian" he and colleagues claimed to be the last survivor of the Yahi people.

name: Alfred Marston Tozzer
write_up: Alfred Marston Tozzer (1877-1954): archaeologist and anthropologist. After earning a PhD from Harvard in cultural anthropology, Tozzer conducted field work internationally, specializing in study of the Maya. He taught at Harvard, served as a curator of the Peabody Museum, and was an officer in both World War I and II. In addition to field work throughout his life, he held various administrative positions at Harvard and in scientific associations, including the American Anthropological Association, the Peabody Museum, and the National Research Council. His published works include A Comparative Study of the Mayas and the Lacandones (1907) and A Grammar of the Maya Language (1921).

name: Alfred Robinson
write_up: Alfred Robinson (1806-95): an agent for the Boston firm Bryant & Sturgis, which traded in California throughout the Mexican period. The firm purchased hides, tallow, and local produce from the ranches and missions, selling or exchanging them for manufactured goods. Robinson, who came from an old New England family, lived in California for many years, becoming wealthy through investments in the trade (he was part owner of the ship Alert, for example). Like other New England merchants in California, he served as an intermediary between cultures, marrying the daughter of a wealthy Californio family. Robinson recorded his experiences in the book Life in California (1846).
notes: Ogden, Adele. 1944. "Alfred Robinson, New England Merchant in Mexican California". California Historical Society Quarterly 23:193-218.

name: Alice Cunningham Fletcher
write_up: Alice Cunningham Fletcher (1845-1923): ethnologist and employee of the US Department of the Interior. Fletcher lived and studied the Omaha people in Nebraska in connection with her work at the Peabody Museum at Harvard. After passage of the Dawes Severalty Act of 1877, a measure designed to detribalize American Indians, Fletcher was appointed as special agent by the US Department of the Interior. She allotted individual land tracts to Omaha families and delivered children to Indian boarding schools at Carlisle and Hampton. She was later assigned as special agent for the Winnebago and Nez Perce tribes. Fletcher was associated with numerous anthropological organizations, including the Anthropological Society of Washington, Archaeological Institute of America, and the American Folklore Society.

name: Allen Light
write_up: Allen Light: African-American seaman from Philadelphia who arrived in Santa Barbara by ship in the 1830s. Light for a time served as part of George Nidever’s sea otter hunting party, where he was known for his excellent marksmanship. He served as a mercenary soldier in Juan Bautista Alvarado’s revolutionary attempt to secede from Mexico; later he became a naturalized Mexican citizen. In the 1840s, he may have operated a grog house/saloon.

name: Alpheus Basil Thompson
write_up: Alpheus Basil Thompson (1795–1869): sea merchant from Maine who was active in the sea otter trade between California and China. Thompson lived in Canton as the representative of his uncle’s Boston firm Wildes & Marshall from 1821-25; he then spent several years in Hawaii managing the Pacific trade. When the hide and tallow business began to eclipse that of furs, Thompson moved to Santa Barbara in 1834 and married into the Californio elite (his wife was the daughter of Carlos Antonio Carrillo, governor of Alta California, 1837-38). He subsequently received a land grant on Santa Rosa Island.
notes: Thomas J. Osborne, Pacific Eldorado: A History of California

name: Alpheus Bull
write_up: Alpheus Bull: no further information has been found excepting his association with the American Anthropological Association and position as Vice President of the California Academy of Sciences during this time.

name: Alphonse Pinart
write_up: Alphonse Pinart (1852-1911): a French linguist and adventurer who had a particular interest in the relationship between the languages of Alaska Natives and the indigenous peoples of Siberia, as he was an early supporter of the idea that the Native peoples of America had ancestral roots in Asia. Pinart traveled around the Americas and parts of Europe studying native languages and collecting, and often stealing, antiquities. Among the places Pinart conducted field research, he spent a year from 1871-2 on the Kodiak and Aleutian Islands studying the languages of the Alutiiq (Sugpiaq) people. Pinart shared his field notes from his Alaskan expedition with California historian and ethnologist, Hubert Howe Bancroft.
notes:
https://www.nps.gov/katm/blogs/explorers-of-katmai-country-alphonse-pinart-1852-1911.htm
https://alutiiqmuseum.org/explore/past-exhibits/280-making-faces
Laronde, Anne-Claire. 2009. “The Atypical History of Collector Alphonse Pinart (1852-1911) and the Sugpiaq Masks of Boulogne-sur-Mer in France”. In Giinaquq Like a Face: Sugpiaq Masks of the Kodiak Archipelago. Sven D. Haakanson, Jr. and Amy F. Steffian, eds. Pp. 31-35. Fairbanks, AK: Universiy of Alaska Press. http://bit.ly/1tDKae1

name: Alvin Seale
write_up: Alvin Seale (1871-1958): a naturalist and ichthyologist, educated at Stanford. Seale was appointed chief of the Division of Fisheries of the Philippine Bureau of Science by President Theodore Roosevelt; here he conducted extensive research and helped develop regulations for the burgeoning industries affecting the fish of that region. Seale was well-known for his design of the Steinhart Aquarium (among others).

name: Andrés Castillero
write_up: Andrés Castillero: a diplomat for the Mexican government. Castillero was sent to Alta California to convince Juan Bautista Alvarado (governor of Alta California) to support the 1836 Mexican Constitution. He succeed in persuading Alvarado that the constitutional reforms would provide California with sufficient autonomy and equitability and prevented immediate war.

name: Andrés Pico
write_up: Andrés Pico (1810–76): ranchero and younger brother of Pio Pico, the last governor of Mexican California. Andrés Pico commanded Mexican forces during the Mexican-American War and briefly served as acting governor of Alta California, in defiance of the US provisional government. Ultimately, Pico signed the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo and, after California became a US state, served in state politics and the state militia.

name: Angel Fish
write_up: Angel Fish: a fish exhibited as a curiosity in one of P.T. Barnum's museums.

name: Antonia María de Jesús
write_up: Antonia María de Jesús: no further information has been found excepting her marriage to Isaac Williams and that she was a daughter of Antonia Lugo.

name: Antonio Garra, Sr.
write_up: Antonio Garra, Sr. (1815?-52): a Cupeño who lived at the San Luis Rey Mission. He organized a Native resistance movement and attacked Warner's Ranch. Garra was captured, turned over to California authorities, convicted of murder and theft, and executed by firing squad.

name: Aravio/Arabio (Talawiyashwit)
write_up: Aravio (1782?-1864): a Chumash from Santa Cruz Island. Aravio (Talawiyashwit) reportedly translated the words of the Lone Woman’s "Toki Toki" song for Fernando Librado, who later shared its meaning, as Aravio understood it, with ethnographer J.P. Harrington. According to J. P. Harrington's notes, Aravio had traveled by tomol (plank canoe) to San Nicolas Island, interacting with the Nicoleños before they left the island in 1835. He therefore may have known some of the Nicoleño language. The name "Aravio" derives from the Chumash pronunciation of his Spanish baptismal name, Braulio.

name: Archer Taylor
write_up: Archer Taylor (1890-1973): an internationally renowned scholar of proverbs, folklore, and the German language who taught at Washington University, University of Chicago, and finally the University of California, Berkeley.  He completed his undergraduate work at Swarthmore College and his graduate studies in German at the University of Pennsylvania (MA) and Harvard University (PhD). Taylor and his wife, Hasseltine Byrd Taylor, lived in Napa Valley on what is now the Archer Taylor Preserve.

name: Armstrong W. Sperry
write_up: Armstrong W. Sperry (1897-1976): an author and illustrator. Sperry served in the United States Navy in World War I. He studied at Yale, the Art Students League in New York, and the Académie Colarossis in Paris, and he worked in advertising before beginning his career as a children's author. His published works include One Day with Manu (1933) and Call It Courage (1940; 1941 Newbery Honor Book).

name: Arthur R. Sanger
write_up: Arthur R. Sanger: a realtor and an amateur archaeologist who collected Indian curios. Sanger began collecting artifacts on San Nicolas Island as early as the 1910s and he continued into the 1940s. In 1926, he was a field assistant for an important dig on the island led by Bruce Bryan and sponsored by the Natural History Museum of Los Angeles.  Sanger befriended Mr. and Mrs. Roy Agee, who managed the sheep ranch operations on San Nicolas Island in the 1930s and 40s. Today, artifacts collected by Sanger are housed at a number of museums, including the National Museum of the American Indian (Smithsonian). However, both his methods and the validity of his claims have been questioned.
notes:
http://islapedia.com/index.php?title=BRYAN,_Bruce

http://www.pcas.org/documents/Dorsalfineffigy.pdf

http://www.mednscience.org/download_product/1625/0

http://articles.latimes.com/1990-03-01/local/me-2319_1_santa-ana

http://www.americanindian.si.edu/searchcollections/item.aspx?irn=217641 unpublished essay by Steve Schwartz (in Schwebel's possession)

name: Arthur Woodward
write_up: Arthur Woodward (1898-1986): an archaeologist. Woodward studied at the University of California, Berkeley under noted California anthropologist Alfred Kroeber and served as a curator for the Los Angeles County Museum (1928-53). While there he contributed extensively to historical archaeological research in California.

name: Asa Gray
write_up: Asa Gray (1810-88): a botanist. Gray attended Fairfield Medical School, was a professor at Harvard University, and edited the American Journal of Science. His seminal work is the Manual of the Botany of the Northern United States (1848, called Gray's Manual).

name: Augustine Hobrecht
write_up: Augustine Hobrecht (1890-1970): a Catholic priest who served as the superior of the Santa Barbara Mission from 1923 to 1931.  During his tenure, the Mission rebuilt after the destruction of the 1925 earthquake in Santa Barbara.

name: Augustus Felix Hinchman
write_up: Augustus Felix Hinchman (1823-99): lawyer, public servant, and manager of mines. Born in Massachusetts and educated at Harvard, Hinchman arrived in California in 1849. He converted to Catholicism and married into the Spanish elite. In the early 1850s, he purchased from George Nidever the land that would later be known as Burton’s Mound.

name: Austin Edwin Fife
write_up: Austin Edwin Fife (1909-86): a researcher, professor, and US Air Force lieutenant. Fife and his wife, Alta, founded the Fife Folklore Collection at Utah State University, where Austin Fife taught for many years.  Fife published widely in the field.

name: Baldwin
write_up: Baldwin: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part.
notes: Weber, David J. The Taos Trappers: The Fur Trade in the Far Southwest, 1540-1846. Norman, Oklahoma: University of Oklahoma Press, 1971.

name: Barker
write_up: Barker: the husband of Nancy Nidever Barker, George Nidever's sister.

name: Barney Brantingham
write_up: Barney Brantingham: journalist and editor. Brantingham worked for numerous newspapers in the Chicago area before editing the San Clemente Sun-Post and later worked as a journalist with the Santa Barbara News-Press (both in California). He won, with others, an Ethics in Journalism Award from the Society of Professional Journalists for his resignation from the News-Press over allegedly unethical management of reporting. He then began writing for The Santa Barbara Independent.

name: Bartolomé Ferrelo
write_up: Bartolomé Ferrelo: chief pilot of Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo's exploration of the United States' western coastline (1542-43). Upon his death, Cabrillo left Ferrelo as captain. Ferrelo subsequently travelled along the Pacific Coast as far north as Oregon.

name: Benjamin Ide Wheeler
write_up: Benjamin Ide Wheeler (1854-1927): philologist, Greek scholar, and college administrator. Wheeler attended Brown University and earned a PhD at Heidelberg University in Berlin. He held teaching positions at Brown and Cornell Universities before serving as President of the University of California. Wheeler later taught at the American School of Classical Studies at Athens and the University of Berlin.

name: Benjamin Ignatius Hayes
write_up: Benjamin Ignatius Hayes (1815-77): a lawyer who moved west and became the first judge of California's Southern District. Throughout his life he kept notes and journals about early California and his experiences in the region. Hubert Howe Bancroft later combined these and published Pioneer Notes from the Diaries of Judge Benjamin Hayes, 1849-1875 (1929).

name: Benjamin Louis Eulalie de Bonneville
write_up: Benjamin Louis Eulalie de Bonneville (1795-1878): a soldier and fur trader. Bonneville was commissioned at the United States Military Academy at West Point and spent much of his career doing reconnaissance of Native American tribes for the Army.  During this time, he also explored the Rocky Mountains. He served during the Civil War, eventually becoming a brigadier general.

name: Bennett Mason Scott
write_up: Bennett Mason Scott (1870-1949): Scott O'Dell's father. A Midwesterner, Scott moved west to California shortly after his marriage and before the birth of his first child. He worked for the Union Pacific Railroad and later owned drug stores in Los Angeles.

name: Bernard Bernardoni
write_up: Bernard Bernardoni (1903-1971): the president of the Galveston Reserve Officers and a first lieutenant of infantry reserves.  He was an economic analyst for the US Department of State in North Africa from 1939 to 1947.

name: Black Hawk
write_up: Black Hawk (?-1845?): a California Indian who lived at San Pedro near Los Angeles, Black Hawk was given this name by the crew of the ship Alert. According to George Nidever and William Dane Phelps, Black Hawk was a native of San Nicolas Island who was brought to the mainland in 1835. Secondary accounts state Black Hawk died from a fall in 1845 and was buried on Deadman Island in San Pedro Bay. In 1928, the island was dredged away as part of improvements to the San Pedro Harbor.

name: Blaise Cronin
write_up: Blaise Cronin (1918-99): a Franciscan priest who spent more than fifty years as a teacher, principal, and chaplain at Saint Mary’s, the oldest Catholic high school in Arizona. As a student friar at the Franciscan School of Theology in Santa Barbara (also known as the "Old Mission Theological Seminary"), Cronin and fellow classmate Kenneth Henriques uncovered the Lone Woman’s baptismal record, which had been obscured in the Santa Barbara Mission Burial Registry for a number of years. Before their rediscovery of the document, historians had relied upon George Nidever’s reporting of the baptism but lacked proof that the baptism had actually taken place.

name: Blanche Trask
write_up: Blanche Trask (1865-1916): poet, author, and botanist. Trask spent two decades gathering plant and archaeological specimens from the Channel Islands.

name: Boris Tasarov
write_up: Boris Tasarov: a Russian otter hunter and fur trader. Tasarov was in charge of a Russian American Company hunting group that was taken to San Nicolas Island on the ship Forester in 1815. He and his hunting crew pursued otters on San Nicolas, Santa Rosa, and Catalina Islands before paddling to San Pedro, on the mainland. They were captured by the Spanish and detailed for illegal hunting in Spanish waters; the men were sent to Santa Barbara and Monterey, California, while Tarasov was sent to Mexico. However, he escaped from Spanish authorities in 1819. Ultimately, Spanish attempts to prevent hunting by foreigners failed, and the otter population was decimated.
notes:
https://www.nps.gov/subjects/islandofthebluedolphins/boris-tarasov.htm
https://daily.jstor.org/juana-maria-blue-dolphins/[SS2] 
Chiles, Frederic Claire. California's Channel Islands: A History, p. 198 U of OK Press, 2015. https://books.google.com/books?id=FDZLBgAAQBAJ&pg=PA198&lpg=PA198&dq=Boris+Tasarov+otter+hunter&source=bl&ots=eWLMrqkAGC&sig=Ir89kdQyaDdHodgBfnLVBuquoig&hl=en&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwiVi967s_XUAhWBHT4KHaYLBlgQ6AEIMDAB#v=onepage&q=Boris%20Tasarov%20otter%20hunter&f=false

name: Bowen
write_up: Bowen: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part.
notes: Weber, David J. The Taos Trappers: The Fur Trade in the Far Southwest, 1540-1846. Norman, Oklahoma: University of Oklahoma Press, 1971.

name: Brandtz Mayer
write_up: Brandtz Mayer (1809-79): a lawyer, writer, historian, and prominent citizen of Baltimore. Mayer attended St. Mary's Seminary and the University of Maryland. After a short stint practicing law, he served as a diplomat to Mexico and began writing extensively. Mayer edited the Baltimore News American, wrote for various newspapers, and helped establish both the Maryland Historical Society and the state's first archive. He served in the Union Army during the Civil War. His published works include Mexico as It Was and as It Is (1844), Mexico, Aztec, Spanish and Republican (1851), and Calvert and Penn (1852).

name: Bright Morning
write_up: Bright Morning: a fictional Navajo girl and the protagonist of Scott O'Dell's Newbery Honor book, Sing Down the Moon (1970).

name: Bruce Bryan
write_up: Bruce Bryan (1906-2004): self-taught anthropologist (high school graduate) whose work concentrated on the American Southwest. He excavated in Arizona, New Mexico, and California, leading an important dig on San Nicolas Island in 1926. This excavation, organized by the Natural History Museum of Los Angeles County, included Charles W. Hatton and Arthur R. Sanger as field assistants. During two significant periods in his career, Bryan was employed by the Southwest Museum in Los Angeles; during the 1960s, he served as editor of the museum’s journal, the Masterkey.
notes: Houlihan, P.T. (1983). Bruce Bryan at the Southwest Museum.  The Masterkey, 57(4). 125-128. Icolari, D. (Ed.). (1973). Reference encyclopedia of the American Indian, 2nd ed., Vol. 2.  Who’s who. Rye, NY (1990): Todd Publications. p. 38; http://islapedia.com/index.php?title=BRYAN,_BRUCE

name: Burke
write_up: Burke: Santiago's account does not give further information on this individual, but it possibly refers to Captain James W. Burke, an Irish sea captain who settled in Santa Barbara in the 1820s.

name: C. E. Rumsey
write_up: C. E. Rumsey: no further information has been found excepting his association with the American Anthropological Association, the American Folklore Society, and the Archaeological Institute of America during this time.

name: C. P. Patterson
write_up: C. P. Patterson (1851-81): a soldier and engineer. Born in Mississippi, Patterson attended Georgetown College, served in the United States Navy and the US Coast Survey, and made his living as a steamship captain during the boom following California's entry into the United States. Patterson later rejoined the Coast Survey, where he remained employed until his death.

name: C.
write_up: C.: person who wrote an anonymous letter about the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island to the Daily Herald (Newburyport, MA). It was later reprinted as "A Female Crusoe" in other newspapers, including the Anti-Slavery Bugle (New Lisbon, OH) and the Evening Star (Washington, DC).

name: Caleb Clegg
write_up: Caleb Clegg: fictional, half-mad sailor in Scott O'Dell's sea novel The Dark Canoe (1968).

name: Campbell Grant
write_up: Campbell Grant (1909-92): an artist and author. Grant attended the California College of Arts and Crafts and the Santa Barbara School of the Arts before spending more than a decade with Walt Disney Studios, working on films like Fantasia, Pinocchio, and Snow White. He taught high school, painted murals with the New Deal's Works Progress Administration and illustrated books before discovering a passion for Native American rock art. Grant is credited with the discovery and subsequent preservation of nearly fifty Native American rock painting sites.

name: Aubrey
write_up: Captain Aubrey: a man who broke a new trail between San Francisco and Santa Fe in 1853, and recommended the route as a possible railroad line of approximately 1000 miles.
notes: SF Daily Herald 5 Dec. 1853, "Captain Aubrey's trip across the Great Basin"

name: Burrows
write_up: Captain Burrows (?-1846): the commander of a detachment of forty-five American volunteers during the Mexican-American War. He and his men were responsible for defeating a group of two hundred Californians at Salinas near Monterey on November 15, 1846. Although it was a complete victory for the Americans, he died at the battle along with three of his men.

name: Captain Daniel A. Hill
write_up: Captain Daniel A. Hill (1797-1865): a California entrepreneur and landowner. Raised on a farm near Boston, Hill left home and served on the Rebecca, heading to Hawaii, in 1822. From there, he became first mate on The Rover, eventually landing in California. Here Hill met the granddaughter of José Francisco Ortega, Rafaela, whom he married in 1825 after converting to Catholicism and becoming a Mexican citizen.

name: Captain E. M. Condra, Jr.
write_up: Captain E. M. Condra, Jr.: the commanding officer of the Pacific Missile Test Center from July 23, 1952 to October 30, 1954.

name: Horatio Gates Trussell
write_up: Captain Horatio Gates Trussell (1814?-80?): a sea captain from Maine listed as a tradesman in the 1852 California census of Santa Barbara. According to George Nidever, Horatio Gates Trussell offered him $1,000 to exhibit the Lone Woman in San Francisco as a circus curiosity.  In his memoir, Nidever states that he turned down Trussell’s offer.

name: Captain John Nightingale
write_up: Captain John Nightingale: a British surgeon-captain. He resigned from his post on March 24, 1897.

name: Romualdo Pacheco, Sr.
write_up: Captain José Antonio Romualdo Pacheco (1795-1831): a Mexican general from Guanajuato who relocated to Alta California in 1825. He married Ramona Carillo, and the couple had a son, Antonio Romualdo Pacheco, Jr., who would become sitting governor of California and a member of the US House of Representatives. Pacheco Sr. was killed protecting Mexican governor Manuel Victoria at the first battle of Cahuenga Pass, only five weeks after Pacheco Jr. was born.
notes:
"PACHECO, Romualdo." US House of Representatives: History, Art & Archives. Accessed March 09, 2017. http://history.house.gov/People/Listing/P/PACHECO,-Romualdo-(P000003)/.
"CPT Jose Antonio Romualdo Pacheco (1795 - 1831) - Find A Grave Memorial." CPT Jose Antonio Romualdo Pacheco (1795 - 1831) - Find A Grave Memorial. Accessed March 24, 2017. https://www.findagrave.com/cgi-bin/fg.cgi?page=gr&GRid=163482694.

name: Marcus Harloe
write_up: Captain Marcus Harloe (1833-1908): a sailor and US statesman. From humble beginnings as an immigrant sailor, Harloe rose to become the Harbor Master and then Chief Wharfinger of San Francisco. He was elected to the California State Legislature as a Republican and served as the supervisor of the Arroyo Grande District for San Luis Obispo County. He was active in his community, serving, for example, as a local school trustee. Harloe married the daughter of Isaac J. Sparks, the man who removed the Nicoleños from San Nicolas Island to the California mainland in 1835.

name: Orlov
write_up: Captain Orlov: the Russian captain of the Aleut otter hunters in Scott O'Dell's Island of the Blue Dolphins.

name: Thomas M. Robbins
write_up: Captain Thomas M. Robbins (1801?-54): mate or master on the Rover, Waverly, Santa Barbara, Pacifico, and California as they traded on the California coast during the first half of the nineteenth century. Robbins told William Dane Phelps that he visited San Nicolas Island and took two women from the island to San Pedro. He also told Phelps that one Nicoleña female was left behind on the island.

name: Carl Dittman
write_up: Carl Dittman (1825-1901): a sailor, hunter, and rancher. Carl Dittman was the first person to find the Lone Woman on San Nicolas Island in 1853, while hunting sea otters with George Nidever and five others. Dittman was also called Charley Brown, a name he was given by an English sea captain. Later, Dittman, who was born in Prussia, became an American citizen. His account of finding the Lone Woman, "Narrative of a Seafaring Life on the Coat of California," was taken by E. F. Murray, a research assistant of Hubert Howe Bancroft.

name: Carl Purdy
write_up: Carl Purdy (1861-1945): a self-educated botanist from Mendocino County, California. His early correspondence with more established botanists regarding native California plant life garnered him a reputation as a respected practitioner. His major success was in the nursery business, selling plants he often bred himself. Purdy wrote and lectured widely on horticulture and native California plant life.

name: Carlos Antonio de Jesus Carrillo
write_up: Carlos Antonio de Jesus Carrillo (1783-1852): a soldier and politician in Alta California. Carrillo served in the Mexican Army and as a member of the Mexican Congress. He was involved in military conflict with then-governor Juan Alvarado, briefly served as the governor of Alta California (1837-38), and, after California became part of the United States, served in Santa Barbara city government until his death.

name: Carmichael
write_up: Carmichael: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part.
notes: Weber, David J. The Taos Trappers: The Fur Trade in the Far Southwest, 1540-1846. Norman, Oklahoma: University of Oklahoma Press, 1971.

name: Catherine W. Hittell
write_up: Catherine W. Hittell: most likely the daughter of Theodore Henry Hittell, a lawyer and Senator who served as editor of the San Francisco Bulletin.

name: Cecilia Rasmussen
write_up: Cecilia Rasmussen: journalist for the Los Angeles Times. Rasmusseun studied business in college and worked in banking before taking a secretarial job at the Los Angeles Times. She progressed to researcher and retired as one of the Times' most popular staff writers, known for her "L.A. Than and Now" columns.

name: Ceaser Lataillade
write_up: Cesario Eugenio Lataillade (1849 - 1938): a Basque immigrant. He settled in Santa Barbara, California, and was a councilman for the city. After California became part of the United States, he anglicized his name to Cesar Eugene.

name: Charles A. Storke
write_up: Charles A. Storke (1847-1936): a lawyer. As a young man, Storke served as a Union soldier in the Civil War. He graduated from Cornell University and then moved to California, where he taught mathematics and Latin at the newly founded Santa Barbara College and was admitted to the bar. Sorke served in the California legislature and founded both the Los Angeles Herald and the Santa Barbara News-Press.

name: Charles C. Willoughby
write_up: Charles C. Willoughby: one of Harvard's earliest professors of anthropology; he was largely self-taught in the field. John Reed Swanton, who completed important ethnographic work in the Pacific Northwest, South, and Southeast, began his studies under Willoughby.

name: Charles Christian Nahl
write_up: Charles Christian Nahl (1818-78): a German painter. Nahl attended the Kassel Academy in Germany and was involved in the 1848 revolutionary movement in France. He eventually settled in San Francisco and became one of California's first acclaimed artists, admired for his romantic depictions of early California citizens and wildlife.

name: C.F. McGlashan
write_up: Charles Fayette "C.F." McGlashan (1847-1931): a California journalist. He was the editor and owner of the Truckee Republican, and publisher of the Santa Barbara Press.
notes:
https://www.findagrave.com/cgi-bin/fg.cgi?page=gr&GRid=28799538 (I just used this for his full name)
http://www.norcalmediamuseum.org/?page_id=432

name: Charles Fletcher Lummis
write_up: Charles Fletcher Lummis (1859-1928): author and activist. After graduating from Harvard College, Lummis worked as a journalist who walked from Ohio to California, writing about the Southwestern US along the way. Later jobs included: city editor of the Los Angeles Times, librarian at the Los Angeles Public Library, and editor of the Land of Sunshine (later Out West Magazine). Lummis was an advocate for Native rights and for the preservation of Southwest history. He founded the Sequoya League (an advocacy group), the Southwest Museum (now the Autry Museum of the American West), and the Landmarks Club (a preservation group); he published almost twenty books.

name: Charles Frederic Newcombe
write_up: Charles Frederic Newcombe (1851-1924): an English physician, naturalist, and collector. Newcombe attended the University of Aberdeen and later studied at the University of London and the British Museum. He worked as a general physician in England and then in North America. Later in his career, Newcombe devoted increasingly more time to natural history, particularly the study of the northwest coast of Canada. He collaborated with Franz Boas and helped to found the Victoria Natural History Society.

name: Charles Frederick Holder
write_up: Charles Frederick Holder (1851-1915): naturalist and sportsman. In 1898, he founded The Tuna Club, a big game fishing club on Catalina Island. Before moving to California for his health, Holder was an assistant curator of zoology at the American Museum of Natural History in New York.
notes:
http://www.igfa.org/Museum/HOF-Holder.aspx 

ENCY Britanica ("Big Game Fishing"); Science, New Series, Vol. 42, No. 1093 (Dec. 10, 1915), pp. 823-825

name: Charles Hill-Tout
write_up: Charles Hill-Tout (1858-1944): an amateur anthropologist who studied the indigenous people of Victoria, Canada and served as the Royal Society of Canada's president of anthropology. His published works include The Native Races of British North America: The Far West (1907). He was the head of a school for boys.

name: Charles Hillinger
write_up: Charles Hillinger (1926-2008): one of the Los Angeles Times's most popular reporters. Hillinger spent his entire career, nearly five decades, with the Times, covering stories including the Beatles' 1964 visit to Los Angeles and Apollo 11's mission to the moon. He traveled throughout the United States and was well known for his portrayals of "local color." During the second half of the 1980s, his syndicated column appeared in more than 600 US newspapers.

name: Charles Hubbard
write_up: Charles Hubbard: a German who became a Mexican citizen. Charles Hubbard was captain of the ship Peor es Nada, chartered by Isaac Sparks for sea otter hunting. Hubbard may have taken the ship to San Nicolas Island in 1835 and returned to San Pedro with the Nicoleños. He was in charge when the ship capsized at the entrance to San Francisco Bay in early 1836. His name was written as Gerard Kuppertz in Mexican California documents.
notes: California's Channel Islands: A History- Frederic Caire Chiles

name: Charles J. W. Russell
write_up: Charles J. W. Russell: oysterman who operated a trading post in Willapa Bay, California during the mid-nineteenth century. Russell hired native Chinook people to harvest oysters for commercial sale and had these transported first by land (over traditional migration routes) and then by ship for sale to gold miners in San Francisco. He knew George Nidever, who related the story of the Lone Woman to him. According to an article in Hutchings’ California Museum, Nidever gave him a number of items owned and/or made by the Lone Woman, including a grass water bottle, necklace, and stone mortar.

name: Charles Keeler
write_up: Charles Keeler (1871-1937): an ornithologist, poet, and passionate advocate for the arts who served as director of the California Academy of Science's Natural History Museum. Keeler was a founding member of the Sierra Club (and as such, as associate of John Muir) and a member of both the American Anthropological Association and American Folklore Society. Keeler Avenue in Berkeley is named in his honor

name: Charles Peabody
write_up: Charles Peabody: most likely the great-nephew of George Peabody, philanthropist and founder of Harvard University’s Peabody Museum.

name: Charles Rozaire
write_up: Charles Rozaire: an archaeologist (PhD, University of California, Los Angeles). He served as a curator for the Southwest Museum and the Los Angeles County Museum of Natural History. Much of his research centers on California's Channel Islands. Published works include Ancient Civilizations of Latin America: An Exhibition of Art and Civilization in Ancient Latin America (1966) and Archaeological Investigations on Anacapa Island, California (1978).

name: Charles W. Hatton
write_up: Charles W. Hatton: a field assistant on an expedition party from the Natural History Museum of Los Angeles County (led by Bruce Bryan) that went to San Nicolas Island in 1926. The party removed 49 unidentified individual's human remains and brought them to the museum for study and display.

name: Charles Webster Merritt
write_up: Charles Webster Merritt (1844-1910): a rancher. Merritt served as Santa Barbara County Supervisor and then superintendent of Santa Rosa Island while it was part of the A. P. More estate.

name: Charles-Brasseur Étienne de Bourbourg
write_up: Charles-Brasseur Étienne de Bourbourg (1814-74): a French historian and Roman Catholic Priest who specialized in Mesoamerican Studies. Despite accusations of plagiarism, de Bourbourg was largely respected for his ethnographic and linguistic studies, which were conducted while traveling Europe and North America for ecclesiastical administrative, teaching, and diplomatic positions. His published works include History of Canada (1852), History of the Civilized Nations of Mexico and Central America (1857-59), and Ancient Monuments of Mexico (1866).

name: Chief Chowig
write_up: Chief Chowig: Karana's father and the tribal chief in Scott O'Dell's novel Island of the Blue Dolphins (1960). He is killed by Aleut hunters.

name: Chinigchinich
write_up: Chinigchinich: a central figure in the mythology of a number of groups of California Mission Indians.

name: Christina Funkhouser Nidever
write_up: Christina Funkhouser Nidever (1777-1827?): mother of George Nidever, the otter hunter who led the party bringing the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island to the California mainland in 1853. She was born in Washington County, Virginia and resettled with her family in Tennessee; she married George Nidever (senior) and with him migrated to North Carolina, Missouri, and finally to Arkansas. They had nine children.

name: Christina Nidever
write_up: Christina Nidever: sister of George Nidever (junior).

name: Christopher Columbus
write_up: Christopher Columbus (1451-1506): Spanish explorer credited with discovering America in 1492.

name: Christopher Houston "Kit" Carson
write_up: Christopher Houston "Kit" Carson (1809-68): a trapper, Indian agent, and soldier in the American West who knew several Native American languages.  He served as a guide for John Charles Frémont's expeditions and was instrumental in the US taking of California under Frémont.  He also served as an Indian Agent in New Mexico, where he organized volunteer forces during the Civil War.
notes:
http://www.oac.cdlib.org/findaid/ark:/13030/tf8s20076p/

http://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Appletons%27_Cyclop%C3%A6dia_of_United States-born_Biography/Carson,_Christopher

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Mexico_Territory_in_the_United States-born_Civil_War
[used to determine CivWar allegience of Carson's unit]

name: Clark Wissler
write_up: Clark Wissler (1870-1947): an anthropologist. Wissler studied psychology at Columbia University—earning his PhD in that field—but became interested in anthropology, in part through contact with Franz Boas. For nearly four decades, Wissler was the curator of ethnology and anthropology at the American Museum of Natural History in New York. He is best remembered for his development of "culture theory," a comparative method of studying Native American populations within a shared geographic region. His published works include Mythology of the Blackfoot Indians (1908), The American Indian (1917), and Man and Culture (1923).

name: Clement Woodward Meighan
write_up: Clement Woodward Meighan (1925-97): an archaeologist and anthropologist (PhD, University of California, Berkeley) who taught at the University of California, Los Angeles throughout his career. As a young man, Meighan was stationed in Hawaii during World War II.

name: Clinton Hart Mirriam
write_up: Clinton Hart Mirriam (1855-1942): a physician, naturalist, and zoologist from New York who was named chief of the Entomological Division of the United States Department of Agriculture (later, the Bureau of Biological Survey). He was a founding member of the American Ornithologists’ Union and National Geographic Society.

name: Colorado
write_up: Colorado: a member of George Nidever’s sea otter hunting group. Colorado assisted George Nidever (Anglo American), Carl Dittman (Prussian), Policarpio (Chumash), Melquiades (Chumash), Hilario Valenzuela (Yaqui) and one unidentified Native man in the search for the Lone Woman on San Nicolas Island in 1853. Colorado’s real name was never recorded. He was an Irishman who received his nickname because of the color of his complexion (colorado is a Spanish word for red or red colored.).
notes: By Frederic Caire Chiles.  JP Harrington notes for names of Native men that are part of 1853 Cora crew

name: Constance Goddard DuBois
write_up: Constance Goddard DuBois (1869-1934): a New England author and self-taught ethnographer who became interested in the plight of Californian Mission Indians (particularly Luiseño, Diegueño/Kumeyaay). In order to advocate for reform, she catalogued first-hand observances of life on Southern California reservations. She was a member of the American Anthropological Association and the American Folklore Society, worked for the American Museum of Natural History, and corresponded with important anthropologists of her day, including Alfred L. Kroeber.

name: D. W. Thompson
write_up: D. W. Thompson: may refer to Dixey (or Dixie) Wildes Thompson, who at the time was engaged in ranching in Santa Barbara and Ventura. Dixey Wildes Thompson was the nephew of Alpheus Basil Thompson and great-great nephew of a principal in the Boston-based Marshall & Wildes firm, which was engaged in trade with China.

name: Daniel Defoe
write_up: Daniel Defoe (1660-1731): an English fiction writer and journalist. Arguably one of the first novelists, Defoe's published works include Robinson Crusoe (1719), the Journal of the Plague Year (1722), and Moll Flanders (1722).

name: Daniel G. Amen
write_up: Daniel G. Amen (1954-): a psychiatrist and author whose research includes brain imaging and study of the rehabilitation of professional football players. Amen is also well-known for his part in constructing the "Daniel Plan," associated with Rick Warren's Saddleback Church in California. His published works include Don't Shoot Yourself in the Foot (1992), Making a Good Brain Great (2005), and Change Your Brain, Change Your Body (2010).

name: Daniel Herbert "D. H." Lawrence
write_up: Daniel Herbert "D. H." Lawrence (1885-1930): a British writer who authored poems, plays, short stories, essays, travel books, letters, and novels, including Sons and Lovers, The Rainbow, and Women in Love.

name: Daniel Hill
write_up: Daniel Hill (1797-1865): a seaman from Boston, Massachusetts. In 1823, he disembarked in California, opened a trading booth, and applied for Mexican citizenship. After converting to Catholicism, he married into the influential Californio family of Don Jose Vicente Ortega. Hill owned a store near the Santa Barbara Mission and worked on construction projects building irrigation systems, houses, and roofing. He eventually became the owner of Rancho La Goleta.

name: Daniel Nidever
write_up: Daniel Nidever: brother of George Nidever (junior).

name: Daniel Shipman
write_up: Daniel Shipman (1801-81): a frontiersman who traveled around Texas with George Nidever in 1822. During the capture of San Antonio in 1835, he led a company of the Texan army in the absence of its commander. He was also one of the Old Three Hundred, the group of settlers who received land grants under Stephen Austin's first colony in the American South/Southwest.

name: David Banks Rogers
write_up: David Banks Rogers (1868-1954): an archaeologist. Rogers worked for the Smithsonian Institution before founding the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History's Department of Anthropology. His published works include Prehistoric Man of the Santa Barbara Coast (1929).

name: David Boyle
write_up: David Boyle (1842-1911): teacher, archaeologist, and author. Boyle moved from Great Britain to Upper Canada as a teen and worked as a teacher for many years, helping to establish a library, school, and natural history organization in Elora, Ontario. He later turned his attention to archaeology fulltime, amassing a sizeable collection of Native artifacts. Though his techniques were rudimentary, he was one of the first researchers in Canada to identify and explore many important sites. Boyle was associated with numerous archaeological and historical organizations.

name: David Rice
write_up: David Rice: archaeologist associated with the Southwest Museum in Los Angeles.

name: Dee Travis Hudson
write_up: Dee Travis Hudson (1941-85): an anthropologist (PhD, Arizona State University) who headed the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History’s Anthropology Department from 1973 until his premature death in 1985. A prolific researcher, Hudson published many articles on California’s indigenous peoples, including the Lone Woman of San Nicolas. He is particularly noted for his work on the Chumash and John P. Harrington’s field notes. Among Hudson’s many publications is a five-volume series on Chumash artifacts and ethnographic objects.
notes: http://escholarship.org/uc/item/7g33r3sq#page-3 ("Memorial to Dee Travis Hudson" J of CA and Great Basin Anthropology 7,2

name: Delaney
write_up: Delaney: a character of Scott O'Dell's book Child of Fire (1974).

name: Dioscorus
write_up: Dioscorus: the father of Saint Barbara. Tradition holds that he was a pagan who detested his daughter's Christian faith and insisted on inflicting the death sentence as punishment.

name: Dixey Wildes Thompson
write_up: Dixey Wildes Thompson (1826-1903): a sailor, rancher, and farmer. Thompson held a number of jobs in ranching and farming before and after a short time working for his uncle, Alpheus B. Thompson, overseeing Santa Rosa Island.

name: Dixey Wildes
write_up: Dixey Wildes: partner with Josiah Marshall in the Boston-based Marshall & Wildes shipping firm, which was engaged in trade with China.

name: Don Alonso Quixote
write_up: Don Alonso Quixote: the titular character of The Ingenious Gentleman Don Quixote of La Mancha (1605-20), the classic picaresque novel by Miguel de Cervantes.

name: Donald Charles Meadows
write_up: Donald Charles Meadows (1897-1994): a biologist and historian. Meadows attended Pomona College and the University of California, Berkeley. He worked as a high school teacher, served as head of the Channel Islands Biological Survey from 1939-40, and was park naturalist for the Big Basin Redwoods State Park and Calaveras Big Trees State Park. During the latter half of his life, he became interested in Southern California history.

name: Clarence E. Edwords
write_up: Dr. Clarence Edgar Edwords (1856-1941): journalist, medical doctor, and author of several books, including one on San Francisco restaurants, Bohemian San Francisco. Edwords was the managing editor and owner of the Journal of Kansas City, Missouri and wrote for several other newspapers. Records indicate that he was a practicing physician, but that the California State Board of Medical Examiners revoked his license in 1930 for performing an illegal operation.

name: James Barron Shaw
write_up: Dr. James Barron Shaw (1814?-1902): a physician who studied in England and Scotland. After moving to California in 1850, he practiced medicine in Santa Barbara. According to Stephen Bowers, an amateur archaeologist who excavated on the California Channel Islands in the late nineteenth century, Dr. Shaw met the Lone Woman on the beach in Santa Barbara when she arrived with George Nidever in 1853. In addition to practicing medicine, Shaw was involved in the management of the sheep ranching operation on Santa Cruz Island.

name: James S. Craig
write_up: Dr. James S. Craig: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part.
notes: Weber, David J. The Taos Trappers: The Fur Trade in the Far Southwest, 1540-1846. Norman, Oklahoma: University of Oklahoma Press, 1971.

name: Samuel B. Brinkerhoff
write_up: Dr. Samuel Bevier Brinkerhoff (1823-80): a New York-born surgeon listed in the 1860 US census for Santa Barbara County; he was living in the same neighborhood as Isaac Sparks and Lewis T. Burton. In her 1880 Scribner’s Monthly article, "Eighteen Years Alone: A Tale of the Pacific," Emma Hardacre states that Dr. Brinkerhoff treated the Lone Woman when she lived with the Nidever family in 1853. A local history reports that Brinkerhoff arrived in Santa Barbara on the Ohio in 1852.
notes:
History of Santa Barbara, California, with illustrations and biographical sketches of its prominent men and pioneers. Oakland: Thompson & West, 1883.  "Biography of Sameul Bevier Brinkerhoff," p.41.   

https://books.google.com/books?id=hXMIAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA41-IA3&lpg=PA41-IA3&dq=samuel+bevier+brinkerhoff&source=bl&ots=TjHUMJ4Br0&sig=LjGYgEcWv3AafUk15nHcee018eU&hl=en&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwi27vPN3brSAhVDKCYKHanjDusQ6AEITzAP#v=onepage&q=samuel%20bevier%20brinkerhoff&f=true

name: Durante "Dante" degli Alighieri
write_up: Durante "Dante" degli Alighieri (1265-1321): an Italian statesman, poet, and satirist. Dante was active in the faction politics of his native Florence and was eventually exiled from the city by his enemies. During this time, he wrote his most famous work, The Divine Comedy.

name: E. G. Blair
write_up: E. G. Blair: president of the San Clemente sheep company sometime before 1925 and indicated as the owner of San Clemente Island sometime before 1930.

name: E. D. Putnam
write_up: E.D. Putnam: elected to the American Anthropologist Association as a new member in 1905. Possibly the daughter of Association President Frederick Ward Putnam (1839-1915).

name: Edgar Lee Hewett
write_up: Edgar Lee Hewett (1865-1947): archaeologist and anthropologist (Doctorate of Science, University of Geneva, 1908). Born in Illinois, Hewett studied the liberal arts at Tarkio College (Missouri) and spent a number of years teaching in country schools and serving as a school superintendent. He became interested in archaeology and began informal fieldwork in New Mexico. When he assumed the presidency of Normal University (New Mexico), he organized archaeological expeditions and began a long and prolific career in that field. In 1906, Hewett was appointed Director of American Research at the Archaeological Institute of America and helped draft the American Antiquities Act, important legislation that provided protection of archaeological sites by naming them National Monuments in the United States. Hewett attended lectures at the University of Geneva beginning in 1903, submitting a dissertation on archaeology in 1908. During the course of his career Hewett was associated with numerous scholarly organizations, including the American Federation of Arts, the American Association for the Advancement of Science, and the Archaeological Institute of America.

name: Edmund Leonard Doran
write_up: Edmund Leonard Doran (1864-1915): a businessman and archaeology enthusiast. Doran was involved in the burgeoning California oil industry and the Santa Catalina Island Meteor Boat Company. Doran was one of the inaugural members of the Santa Catalina Tuna Club. No reports survive of his archaeological endeavors, but items retrieved by him from Santa Catalina Island are in the Museum of the American Indian in Washington, DC.

name: Edward F. Murray
write_up: Edward F. Murray: a research assistant for historian Hubert Howe Bancroft. Between 1876-77, Murray made copies of the archival holdings of the Santa Barbara Mission. In 1878, twenty-five years after the Lone Woman’s removal from San Nicolas, Murray recorded—in elegant longhand—George Nidever and Carl Dittman’s memoirs, including their descriptions of the events surrounding the Lone Woman’s removal to mainland California.
notes: Google e-book: Harry Clark, An Adventure in History: The Production, Preparation, and Sale of the Works of Hubert Bancroft, p17; Scientific a US-born Supplement, vol. 34

name: Edward Fitzgerald Beale
write_up: Edward Fitzgerald Beale (1822-93): a rancher, soldier, and politician who became a multi-millionaire. Beale served in the United States Navy under Robert F. Stockton during the Mexican-American War and was later appointed the first Superintendent of Indian Affairs for California and Nevada. Beale became famous when he carried California gold nuggets east in 1848, helping to trigger the California Gold Rush. He was appointed by President James Buchanan to survey and build a road that facilitated wagon travel west, a route that later became part of Route 66, the Santa Fe Railroad, and Interstate 40. The survey expedition was notable for the US Army's use of camels to carry supplies.

name: Edward Lee Greene
write_up: Edward Lee Greene (1843-1915): a botanist who named more than four thousand new species of plants. His library and collections are held at the University of Notre Dame’s Greene-Nieuwland Herbarium.

name: E.P. Ripley
write_up: Edward Payson Ripley (1845-1920): fourteenth president of the Atchison, Topeka & Santa Fe Railway. Born in Dorchester, Massachusetts, he entered the railway service in 1865. He was named president of the company on January 1, 1896, and is remembered for improving the physical conditions of the railroad. He held his post until 1920.
notes:
Armitage, Merle (1973). Homage to the Santa Fe; The many facets of big time railroading (reprinted 1986 ed.). Hawthorne, California: Omni Publications. p. 139.
"Edward Payson Ripley." Find a Grave. Accessed March 06, 2017. https://www.findagrave.com/cgi-bin/fg.cgi?page=gr&GRid=7889588.
Railway Age Gazette. Vol. 59. Simmons-Boardman Publishing Company, 1959.
International Railway Journal. Vol. 8. The New York Public Library, 1900.

name: Edward Payson Roe, Sr.
write_up: Edward Payson Roe, Sr. (1838-88): an author. His published works include From Jest to Earnest (1875) and He Fell in Love with His Wife (1886).

name: Edward Richard Sprigg Canby
write_up: Edward Richard Sprigg Canby (1817-73): a soldier. Canby attended the United States Military Academy at West Point, served in the Seminole and Mexican Wars, fought in the Union Army during the Civil War, and was one of the US generals to accept Confederate surrender at the conclusion of the conflict.

name: Edward Vail
write_up: Edward Vail: a rancher and San Nicolas Island lessee from 1919-34. He hired Lyman and Edna Elliott as caretakers for the herds of sheep on San Nicolas Island. Vail was one of the seven sons of Walter L. Vail, partner in the Vail & Vickers Company (established in 1899) that moved from Arizona to Southern California and held extensive tracks of land through successive generations until the end of 2011.

name: Edwin M. Sheridan
write_up: Edwin M. Sheridan (1854-1941): a journalist, editor, and publisher. Sheridan entered the printing trade as a young man and was involved in a number of California publications including the Ventura Signal, San Francisco Bulletin, San Francisco Chronicle, and San Francisco Post. As a journalist, he wrote for the San Francisco Bulletin and Call, Oakland Times, Enquirer, Los Angeles Herald, Ventura Free Press, and Oxnard Sun. He later directed the news bureaus of the San Francisco Call and San Francisco Chronicle. Sheridan also collected curios, and for more than two decades he served as a curator at the Ventura County Museum. His published works include the History of Santa Barbara, San Luis Obispo and Ventura Counties, California (1917) and the History of Ventura County, State of California (1940).

name: Elizabeth Hall
write_up: Elizabeth Hall: librarian, editor, author. During the late 1960s, Hall joined the staff of the then brand-new, magazine Psychology Today, where she became managing editor and interviewed the "who’s who" of psychology, including Jean Piaget, Barbel Inhelder, and B. F. Skinner. A few years earlier, while still a librarian, she had met Scott O’Dell, who at the time was becoming famous as the author of Island of the Blue Dolphins (1960). They became partners and, many years later, husband and wife. After O’Dell’s death in 1989, Hall completed and published his unfinished novel Thunder Rolling in the Mountains (1992). She then went on to write and publish a number of children’s books of her own while also administering the Scott O’Dell Award for Historical Fiction. O'Dell's novel Sarah Bishop (1980), set in New York, was written after Psychology Today—and Elizabeth Hall and Scott O’Dell—moved its headquarters from California to New York.

name: Elizabeth Mason
write_up: Elizabeth Mason (1880-1953): a Santa Barbara artist and local historian. She was the niece of Emma Hardacre, author of "Eighteen Years Alone," a widely cited article about the Lone Woman. Mason studied at the New York School of Design and the Pratt Institute. From 1929-42, she worked with the famed anthropologist J. P. Harrington to create 28 exhibit dioramas of Native Californian peoples for the Southwest Museum (now part of the Autry National Center). In 1950, Mason wrote a preface for the reprinting of Hardacre’s "Eighteen Years Alone" in booklet form.

name: Emma Chamberlain Hardacre
write_up: Emma Chamberlain Hardacre (1844? -1930): a journalist who traveled widely and wrote for a number of newspapers in the Midwest and East, including the Louisville Courier-Journal, Chicago Times, and New York Herald. Her article about the Lone Woman, published in Scribner’s Monthly in 1880, was widely reprinted and treated as an authoritative source. After Hardacre’s grown daughter settled in California, Hardacre moved to Santa Barbara. Here, she was acknowledged as a local expert on the Lone Woman.
notes:
The Land of Sunshine/
http://books.google.com/books?id=m3gLAQAAIAAJ&pg=PA284&lpg=PA284&dq=Louisville+Courier+Journal+emma+hardacre&source=bl&ots=FsHVB0utQu&sig=9QtLmhPx-wzDICbhuDe1nVa5ykI&hl=en&sa=X&ei=o1gjU5-uFKKW0QHniIHADQ&ved=0CDkQ6AEwAA#v=onepage&q=Louisville%20Courier%20Journal%20emma%20hardacre&f=false

"Historical and biographical record of southern California; containing a history of southern California from its earliest settlement to the opening year of the twentieth century"

http://interactive.ancestry.com/25488/dvm_LocHist010546-00301-1/663?backurl=http%3a%2f%2fsearch.ancestry.com%2fcgi-bin%2fsse.dll%3fgst%3d-6&ssrc=&backlabel=ReturnSearchResults&rc=2531,518,2834,567;490,805,770,855;866,798,1332,851;788,868,1124,919;2078,915,2396,965;2696,1112,3005,1164;2591,2106,2904,2157;1782,2175,2058,2226;468,2267,799,2320;1327,2452,1687,2507;480,3066,790,3117;424,4132,726,4182;1002,4321,1323,4372;1928,4304,2248,4354#?imageId=dvm_LocHist010546-00301-1The Land of Sunshine/

name: Ephraim George Squier
write_up: Ephraim George Squier (1821-88): a journalist, diplomat, amateur archaeologist, and publisher. Squier engaged in archaeological research as he traveled for his journalistic and diplomatic career. While in Chillicothe, Ohio, during the 1840s, for example, he conducted excavations in the Mississippi Valley mounds in collaboration with local physician Edwin Hamilton Davis. This led to the largest collection of mound artifacts yet uncovered, and the results were published by the Smithsonian. In 1863, Squier was appointed US commissioner to Peru, where he studied Incan remains. He edited numerous publications, including Frank Leslie’s Pictorial History of the American Civil War (1861-62).

name: Eric Hoffer
write_up: Eric Hoffer (1902-83): an author. Hoffer was a self-educated laborer, serving as a migrant farm worker and longshoreman. He began publishing in the 1950s, offering powerful insights into mass movements. His published works include The True Believer (1951), The Ordeal of Change (1963), and Before the Sabbath (1979). Hoffer received the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1983.

name: Eric von Ebrochtal
write_up: Eric von Ebrochtal: Scott O'Dell's German shepherd.

name: Esteban de Sandoval
write_up: Esteban de Sandoval: a young cartographer in Scott O'Dell's The King's Fifth (1966). Esteban recounts the story of adventurers seeking the Seven Golden Cities while awaiting trial for withholding the King's fifth of the gold found during said adventures.

name: Eusebius Joseph Molera
write_up: Eusebius Joseph Molera (1846-1932): an engineer who trained at the Royal Academy of Engineering in Madrid. After immigrating to the United States, he was elected president of the California Academy of Sciences. He was also a member of the Astronomical Society of the Pacific.

name: Evelyn Margaret Foster
write_up: Evelyn Margaret Foster (1910-2003): manager of a sheep estate on San Nicolas Island in the late 1930s, along with her husband Reginald H. Lamberth.

name: Ewing Young
write_up: Ewing Young (1799?–1841): mountain man, trapper, and Oregon pioneer. Born in Tennessee, Young trapped beaver along the Gila and Colorado Rivers, hunted sea otter on the California coast, and transported horses and cattle between New Mexico and California and between California and Oregon, creating the Willamette Cattle Company. In doing so, he solidified important trade routes and made settlers in the Pacific Northwest less dependent upon the (British) Hudson’s Bay Company. Young settled in Oregon Territory in the 1830s, establishing a large ranch and trading post.
notes: Thomas J. Osborne, Pacific Eldorado: A History of California ;http://arizonaoddities.com/2012/01/ewing-young-the-southwests-premier-mountain-man/;http://www.oregonhistorictrailsfund.org/trails/ewing-young-route-1834-1837/

name: Expedition leader
write_up: Expedition leader: King's account does not give further information on this individual but he may be referring to either Vitus Bering or Aleksey Ilich Chirikov, both of whom captained ships on the Great Northern Expedition to map a northern route between Russia and North America.

name: Ezekiel Elliott
write_up: Ezekiel Elliott (1833-1912): Elliott and his son, Joseph Vincent Elliott, held the rights to Anacapa Island from 1828-97.

name: F. A. Stuart
write_up: F. A. Stuart: a resident of Palo Alto, California, who was part of the three-woman committee responsible for the creation of Historic Facts and Fancies, a book of state history published in 1907 by the Federated Women's Clubs of California.

name: F. W. Bowden
write_up: F. W. Bowden: printer and publisher of The Public Ledger (based in Newfoundland) from 1869 to the newspaper’s end in 1882. He also published The Indicator, a satirical account of local news, from 1873 to 1878.

name: Father Antonio de la Ascensión
write_up: Father Antonio de la Ascensión: one of the three Carmelite priests on Vizcaíno's expedition along the California coast. His account of the voyage was widely read.

name: Antonio Jimeno
write_up: Father Antonio Jimeno (1802?-76): a Mexican-born Franciscan priest who was in residence at Mission Santa Barbara between 1828 and 1859 (the mission was secularized during this period, in 1834).  Jimeno was living at the mission when the Lone Woman came to the city in 1853 onboard George Nidever’s schooner. Jimeno visited the Lone Woman while she was alive and may have helped to locate indigenous people from Missions San Fernando and Santa Ynez to try to communicate with her.  In 1859, the priest left Santa Barbara, returning to San Fernando College in Mexico City.
notes: Californio Voices-  José Mariá Amador, Lorenzo Asisara, Gregorio Mora-Torres; The Franciscans in California- Zephyrin Engelhardt

name: Fauntleroy
write_up: Fauntleroy: probably Robert H. Fauntleroy, an engineer who worked for the United States Coast Survey.

name: Fenella
write_up: Fenella: a character in Sir Walter Scott's novel Peveril of the Peak (1823). Fenella plays at being deaf and dumb in order to spy on her employer.

name: Fernando Librado (Kitsepawit)
write_up: Fernando Librado (1839-1915): a Ventureño Chumash who was one of ethnographer J. P. Harrington’s key Chumash informants. Fernando Librado (also known by his Native name Kitsepawit) shared stories about Native people who interacted with the Lone Woman in 1853. He learned the Lone Woman’s Toki Toki song from Melquiades, a Chumash man who heard the Lone Woman sing the song in 1853. In 1913, two years before Librado's death, he performed the song for Harrington, who recorded it on a wax cylinder. Librado also acquired a piece of sewn grass and two bone needles that had belonged to the Lone Woman.

name: Frances Agee
write_up: Frances “Frankie” Agee (1927-2001): one of the few children who resided on San Nicolas Island in the 1930s. The Agee family moved to the island to oversee a sheep ranch in 1929, when Frances was three years old. Frances attend a one-room school house on the island

name: Francis Bret Harte
write_up: Francis Bret Harte (1836-1902): author, essayist, humorist, and critic. Harte was known for his "local color" descriptions of life in the American West, which were gritty and honest. He is best known for "The Luck of Roaring Camp" (1868), a short story about life in the California gold mines published in Overland Monthly, a magazine he edited. Later in life, Harte served as US Consul in various European countries.

name: Francis Fletcher
write_up: Francis Fletcher: chaplain on Sir Francis Drake's expedition to circumnavigate the globe. Fletcher wrote an account of the expedition, The World Encompassed by Sir Francis Drake (1854).

name: Francisca
write_up: Francisca: the daughter of Carlos Carillo. Francisca married Alpheus B. Thompson.

name: Francisco de la Guerra
write_up: Francisco Antonio Maria de Altagracia Guillermo de la Guerra (1817-78): the third son of José Antonio Julian de la Guerra y Noriega and Maria Antonia Juliana Carrillo, members of two important Californio families. Born in Santa Barbara during the Spanish era, Guerra became a loyal US citizen after statehood and was the first elected mayor of Santa Barbara, which was incorporated in 1850.
notes: Bancroft's Pioneer Registry;Histor of Santa Barbara County California by J.D. Mason; Historical and Biographical Record of Souther California, by James Miller Guinn; baptismal record

name: Francisco García Diego y Moreno
write_up: Francisco García Diego y Moreno (1785-1845): the first Catholic Bishop of California. When the Mexican government ousted Spanish friars from California, Diego was among the Mexican Franciscans sent to replace them. He petitioned the Catholic Church to name a bishop for the region and was appointed the first Bishop of Baja and Alta California. He also opened the first seminary on the western coast of the United States, where the Santa Inez Mission had been.

name: Francisco Sánchez
write_up: Francisco Sánchez (1813-84): a priest stationed at Mission Santa Barbara when the Lone Woman arrived from San Nicolas Island in 1853. He and other mission priests visited the Lone Woman and brought Native Californians who spoke different languages to the Nidever house in an effort to communicate with her. When the Lone Woman was near death, Father Sánchez conditionally baptized her with the name Juana Maria.

name: Francisco Vázquez de Coronado y Luján
write_up: Francisco Vázquez de Coronado y Luján (1510-54): an explorer and Spanish governor in New Spain (present-day Mexico). Coronado was commissioned to find the mythical Seven Cities of Cíbola for the Spanish government and, in the process, explored areas of what is now the southwestern and midwestern United States. Nonetheless, the expedition was deemed a failure and Coronado returned to his governorship fallen from grace.

name: Frank Fenenga
write_up: Frank Fenenga (1917-94): an archaeologist. Fenenga attended the University of Chicago before transferring to Sacramento Junior College and the University of California, Berkeley, where he completed his BA After the war, Fenenga worked for the Smithsonian’s River Basin Surveys and the University of California Archaeological Survey. He then became a research associate at the University of Nebraska, editing The Plains Anthropologist. In 1965, he returned to California and became an associate professor of anthropology at California State University, Long Beach. He and his students engaged in "salvage archaeology," digs at sites marked for imminent development.

name: Frank M. Spooner
write_up: Frank M. Spooner: the editor of the Brookfield Courier, a nonpartisan four-page newspaper founded in the mid-1970s located in Brookfield, New York.

name: Franklin Harrison Gassaway
write_up: Franklin Harrison Gassaway (?-1923): a journalist and poet who also published under the name Derrick Dodd. Gassaway was an agent of the United States Mint and worked for The San Francisco Examiner for more than three decades.
notes:
Franklin Harrison Gassaway, Poet.
New York Times (1923-Current file) 
[New York, N.Y] 23 May 1923: 21.

https://archive.org/details/pseudonymsautho01hayngoog

https://archive.org/details/waspvol61881unse

name: Franz Boas
write_up: Franz Boas (1858-1942): pioneer of modern anthropology who trained a generation of scholars in the field. Boas taught at Columbia University for four decades after being hired as the school’s first professor of anthropology. (Born in Germany, Boas himself held a PhD in physics with a minor in geography from the University of Kiel). Boas is best known for bringing rigorous scientific research to the field, debunking the concept of racial determinism, and pioneering the use of dioramas for museum display.

name: Franz Hugo Hesse
write_up: Franz Hugo Hesse: served as Prussian privy counselor and was involved with Prussian colonial endeavors in Central America during this time.

name: Kenneth Henriques
write_up: Frater Kenneth Henriques: Catholic priest. As a student friar at the Franciscan School of Theology at Santa Barbara, California (the "Old Mission Theological Seminary") he and fellow classmate Blaise Cronin uncovered the Lone Woman’s baptismal record, which had been obscured in the Santa Barbara Mission Burial Registry for a number of years. Before their rediscovery of the document, historians had relied upon George Nidever’s reporting of the baptism but lacked proof that the baptism had actually taken place.
notes: see entry for Cronin; can't find anything about life after student work

name: Juan Agustín Morfi
write_up: Fray Juan Agustín Morfi (1776-83): a Spanish Franciscan friar. Morfi accompanied Teodoro de Croix on an inspection of New Spain's provinces, including parts of present-day Texas and the United States-Mexico border. He later wrote about the expedition, as well as the history of Texas, for which he is best known.

name: Frazier
write_up: Frazier: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part. He continued west to California.
notes: Weber, David J. The Taos Trappers: The Fur Trade in the Far Southwest, 1540-1846. Norman, Oklahoma: University of Oklahoma Press, 1971.

name: Fred Spalding Witter
write_up: Fred Spalding Witter: a newspaper correspondent and Congregational minister in late nineteenth century San Francisco.
notes:
American Home Missionary Society, The Home Missionary, Volume 58, page 234, Bible House Astor Place,1886.

https://books.google.com/books?id=ZPbNAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA234&lpg=PA234&dq=Fred+Spalding+Witter+san+francisco&source=bl&ots=wnD7QaThj-&sig=9u3Wxj2zfiV-1mWygfr5aSh0uzQ&hl=en&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwjvvsXRwcXSAhVCziYKHT34AVAQ6AEIQjAI#v=onepage&q=Fred%20Spalding%20Witter%20san%20francisco&f=false

Congregational Year-Book, page 342, Boston: Congregational Publishing Society, 1888. 

https://books.google.com/books?id=0gARAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA342&lpg=PA342&dq=Fred+Spalding+Witter+san+francisco&source=bl&ots=pdZP9iPKr8&sig=2RgKmpzf1igIVqvWnPBEtys7XGg&hl=en&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwjvvsXRwcXSAhVCziYKHT34AVAQ6AEIRDAJ#v=onepage&q=Fred%20Spalding%20Witter%20san%20francisco&f=false

name: Frederic Ward Putnam
write_up: Frederic Ward Putnam (1839-1915): anthropologist trained at the Lawrence Scientific School at Harvard. Putnam held many administrative and curatorial positions including ones at the Essex Institute, Boston Society of Natural History, and both Harvard's Museum of Comparative Zoology and Peabody Museum.
