After this name, which is Latin for "light bearer," was used by John Milton in Paradise Lost, the phrase "as proud as Lucifer" became common.

name: Lucy A. Brinkerhoff
write_up: Lucy A. Brinkerhoff (née Noyes): a Massachusetts woman who married Dr. Samuel Bevier Brinkerhoff in 1877. Brinkerhoff was a New York-born surgeon who settled in Santa Barbara and who, according to Emma Hardacre, treated the Lone Woman when she was living with the Nidever family in 1853. Lucy A. Brinkerhoff survived her husband when he passed in 1880.

name: Luis Antonio Maria Ortega
write_up: Luis Antonio Maria Ortega (1855-1927): a contemporary of Jacob Nidever (George Nidever’s son). Ortega was born in Rancho Refugio to a family that had been one of the wealthiest in Mexican California. When he spoke to J. P. Harrington about the Lone Woman, Ortega told Harrington that she had been brought to the home of Jacob’s parents after her arrival in Santa Barbara.

name: Luisa Ygnacio
write_up: Luisa Ygnacio (1836-1922): one of J. P. Harrington's Barbareño Chumash informants. She was married to Policaprio, who met the Lone Woman on San Nicolas Island as part of George Nidever's otter hunting part.  Luisa Ygnacio told Harrington that she thought the Lone Woman would have lived longer had she stayed with the Chumash in Santa Barbara instead of living with the Nidever family. Luisa Ygnacio also mentioned that two Chumash women, Martina (also known by her Native name, Lekte) and Pilar, gathered clams and brought them to the Lone Woman so that she had familiar food to eat. According to notes written by her granddaughter, Luisa Ygnacio met the Lone Woman in Santa Barbara and tried to speak with her, but was unable to communicate with her.

name: Luther Burbank
write_up: Luther Burbank (1849-1926): a horticulturist. Burbank's methods of cross-breeding plants resulted in over 800 new types, including the Idaho potato and numerous strains of lilies.

name: Lyman Elliott
write_up: Lyman P. Elliott (1892-1949): a sheep rancher on San Nicolas Island. Elliott and his family moved to San Nicolas in 1929, overseeing the large sheep herd on the island for a number of years.

name: Lynn S. Atkinson
write_up: Lynn S. Atkinson (1893-1925?): a contractor and engineer instrumental in the building of the Coolidge Dam in Arizona.  He was head of the Atkinson Construction Company, based in Los Angeles.
notes: Jackson, Donald. Building the Ultimate Dam: John S. Eastwood and the Control of Water in the West. Chicago, Illinois: University of Oklahoma Press, 2005: 211, 313. (Reprint)

name: M. E. Dudley
write_up: M. E. Dudley: an Anglo-Californian writer who was active in historic preservation of the Spanish California past, including efforts to build El Camino Real. Her periodical writing appeared under the name Mrs. M. E. Dudley or M. E. Dudley in the Argonaut (in San Diego), Los Angeles Times, Ventura Daily Democrat, and Overland Monthly. She is likely Mary Elizabeth Dudley, born in 1853.
notes:
Phoebe S. Kropp, California Vieja: Culture and Memory in a Modern American Place (Berkeley: University of California Press, 206): 63-64; 87-88
The Planter and Sugar Manufacturer , vol 27, p. 332 http://books.google.com/books?id=LTA_AQAAMAAJ&pg=PA332&lpg=PA332&dq=%22M.+E.+Dudley%22+los+angeles+times&source=bl&ots=2I1px4YZP8&sig=D4Qf2vIreG-wfpzTzwTlXNZ23Ic&hl=en&sa=X&ei=2BGOU-enFoXjsASzlIG4AQ&ved=0CGYQ6AEwBg#v=onepage&q=M.%20E.%20Dudley&f=false

name: M. L. LaMoreaux
write_up: M. L. LaMoreaux: no further information has been found excepting her association with the American Anthropological Association and her position as President of the Texas Anthropological Association during this time.

name: Mafatu
write_up: Mafatu: the protagonist of Armstrong W. Sperry's Newbery Honor book, Call It Courage (1940).

name: Malcolm Rogers
write_up: Malcolm Jennings Rogers (1890-1960): an archaeologist who worked in southern California and Arizona. Rogers studied geology at Syracuse University, but never earned a degree. He spent several weeks on San Nicolas Island in 1929-30, excavating archaeological sites, and from 1930 to 1945, Rogers served as the curator and director for the San Diego Museum of Man.

name: Melquiades
write_up: Melquiades (1815-71): a Chumash man who lived in Ventura, California and who accompanied George Nidever on the 1853 hunting trip that brought the Lone Woman from San Nicolas Island to Santa Barbara. Melquiades memorized the words of a song he heard the Lone Woman sing, the Toki Toki song, and performed it for others. In 1913, more than half a century after the Lone Woman's death, a Chumash man named Fernando Librado sang the Toki Toki song for ethnographer J. P. Harrington, who recorded it onto a wax cylinder.
notes: Harrington notes research in progress (forthcoming publication by Susan Morris, John Johnson, and Steve Schwartz). Travis Hudson, Breath of the Sun (Banning: Malki Museum Press, 1980): 149; Travis Hudson, “Recently Discovered Accounts Concerning the ‘Lone Woman’ of SNI,” Journal of CA and Great Basin Anthropology 3 (1981): 190-91; 1852 CA State census, Santa Barbara County

name: Manuel Castillo
write_up: Manuel Castillo: a Chicano boy and the protagonist of Scott O'Dell's Child of Fire (1974).

name: Marcos Ramón Nidever
write_up: Marcos (Mark) Ramón Nidever (1842-1909): the first-born son of George Nidever and Sinforosa Sanchez de Nidever.

name: María Aleja
write_up: María Aleja: an Island Indian who was baptized when she was about 45 years old as María Aleja at the Los Angeles Plaza Church in July 1836. She is highly likely to be one of the San Nicolas Island Natives who sailed from the island to San Pedro in 1835. Her baptism occurred shortly after the arrival of the schooner Peor es Nada, which brought the Nicoleños to San Pedro. Her godmother was María Ignacia Amador.

name: María del Refugio Francisca Nidever
write_up: María del Refugio Francisca Nidever (1844-69): the older of two daughters born to George Nidever and Sinforosa Sanchez de Nidever. She was nine years old when the Lone Woman lived in the Nidever home.
notes: The Royal Presidio of Santa Barbara, "Deaths, Book 1, 1782-1873," p. 772, no. 1964, Refugio Nidever burial, 4 February 1869; Santa Barbara Mission Archive-Library, Santa Barbara, California.

name: María Josefa Dana
write_up: María Josefa Dana (1812-83?): a member of the Californio elite, her marriage to the Boston merchant William Goodwin Dana (after his conversion to Catholicism and naturalization as a Mexican citizen) helped Dana to establish himself in California. María Josefa was the daughter of Carlos Carillo, provincial governor of Alta California, 1837-38.

name: Maria Ramona Sinforosa Sanchez de Nidever
write_up: María Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez de Nidever (1812-92): the Santa Barbara-born woman who married George Nidever on February 13, 1841. The couple had four sons and two daughters. According to her husband’s account, Sinforosa looked after the Lone Woman while she lived with the Nidever family, and she may have nursed her when the Lone Woman became ill. She would have spoken to the Lone Woman in Spanish.

name: Marion Grace Hollenbach Saunders
write_up: Marion Grace Hollenbach Saunders (1923-98): archaeologist who was employed by the Los Angeles County Museum of Natural History. She was an assistant to Arthur Woodward on the 1940 research trip to San Nicolas Island and other research endeavors. During World War II, Hollenbach was a member of WAVE. After her marriage to Allan Saunders, whom she met while teaching at Smith College's WAVE training school, she moved to Hawaii and was involved in numerous civic activities. Ultimately, however, her career was overshadowed by that of her husband, who was a longtime professor and administrator at the University of Hawaii.
notes:
http://www.arizonahistoricalsociety.org/wp-content/upLoads/library_Woodward-Arthur.pdf

http://www.abaa.org/book/337236157

http://www.mednscience.org/download_product/1514/0; Allan Sauners: The Man and His Legacy; Cultural Negotiations: The Role of Women in Americanist Archeology (2013); http://the.honoluluadvertiser.com/article/2002/Apr/21/op/op06a.html

name: Mark Nidever
write_up: Mark Nidever: brother of George Nidever. Mark was born in Tennessee and accompanied George on the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition. He was killed in a skirmish with Indians.
notes: Weber, David J. The Taos Trappers: The Fur Trade in the Far Southwest, 1540-1846. Norman, Oklahoma: University of Oklahoma Press, 1971.

name: Markoosie Patsang
write_up: Markoosie Patsang: an Inuit author. His published works include Harpoon Hunter (1970).

name: Marla Daily
write_up: Marla Daily: president of the Santa Cruz Island Foundation and editor of Islapedia, an extensive online resource on the California Channel Islands.

name: Marlow
write_up: Marlow: character in and narrator of Joseph Conrad's novel, Lord Jim (1900).

name: Martin Morse Kimberly
write_up: Martin Morse Kimberly (1826-78): a sailor, rancher, and otter hunter. Kimberly lived on Santa Cruz for a time before establishing a sheep ranch on San Nicolas Island. After a period of drought ruined that business, he established a store in Santa Barbara, which he later sold to take up otter hunting in the Pacific Northwest.

name: Martín Violín
write_up: Martín Violín (1840?-1914?): a Gabrielino (Tongva) whose father came from Santa Catalina Island. Violín told Fernando Librado, J. P. Harrington's Ventureño Chumash informant, about the different people who lived on the southern Channel Islands and the languages they spoke. Violín’s father, according to Violín’s half-brother Sétimo Moraga, was said to have transported people from one of the islands by canoe.
notes: See CINP reference list: Hudson, McCawley

name: Martina Lekte/Lekete
write_up: Martina Lekte/Lekete (1810?-?): a Cruzeño Chumash woman who, according to J.P. Harrington informant Luisa Ygnacio, walked with Pilar, a Ventureño Chumash, to the Nidever house in 1853, when the Lone Woman was living there. They brought clams, which they gathered along the way, as a gift for the Lone Woman. Martina thought the Lone Woman spoke like a Fernandeño, the name given to Native peoples who lived at Mission San Fernando, south of Santa Barbara.

name: Mary Elizabeth Scott
write_up: Mary Elizabeth Scott (1873-1947): Scott O'Dell's mother. She was born and raised in Columbus, Ohio, and moved to southern California shortly after her marriage to Bennett Mason Scott.
notes: David L. Russell's Scott O'Dell; 1900 US Federal Census. http://books.google.com/books?id=KIESL4B9dWkC&pg=PA14&lpg=PA14&dq=%22Bennett+Mason+Scott%22&source=bl&ots=y8o5L0T7ck&sig=1ZOFVhC1tuCkOYdlC-cbPotl1ZE&hl=en&sa=X&ei=6WmXU_T8PJKLyASF-ILwDw&ved=0CDQQ6AEwAw#v=onepage&q=%22Bennett%20Mason%20Scott%22&f=false

name: Matilda Coley Griffing Bancroft
write_up: Matilda Coley Griffing Bancroft (1848-?): the second wife of Hubert Howe Bancroft, historian for whom the University of California’s library is named.

name: Maud Hart Lovelace
write_up: Maud Hart Lovelace (1892-1980): an author. Lovelace attended the University of Minnesota and worked as a journalist before writing fiction full-time. Lovelace's most enduring work, the beloved Betsy-Tacy series, began as bedtime stories about her own life, told to her daughter.

name: Max Uhle
write_up: Max Uhle (1856-1944): a German philologist and archaeologist. Uhle was curator of the Dresden Museum and conducted field research in South America for the Universities of Pennsylvania and California. His work there, including a detailed stratigraphic excavation procedure, was important to the study of pre-Columbia South America; his chronology of the region was not displaced until late in the twentieth century.
notes: Oxford Art Online 2009, JOhn Howland Rowe, "Uhle, Max (1856-1944), arhceologist," Oxford U Press, 1884446051

name: Maximus I
write_up: Maximus I: may refer to Roman Emperors Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximainus, Maximus Thrax, or even Maximus Daia. The veneration of Saint Barbara occurred after her lifetime supposedly occurred, making it difficult to link figures within her story to specific individuals.

name: Maynard J. Geiger
write_up: Maynard J. Geiger (1901-77): a Roman Catholic priest, historian, and archivist. Geiger attended St. Anthony's Seminary and the Catholic University of America. He spent four decades as the archivist of the Santa Barbara Mission and devoted his career to preserving and defending the historic California missions and mission system. Geiger's published works include The Life and Times of Fray Junipero Serra, O.F.M. (1959) and Franciscan Missionaries in Hispanic California, 1768-1848: A Biographical Dictionary (1969).

name: Michael J. Phillips
write_up: Michael J. Phillips: a short-story writer and Air Force veteran. He was the editor of the Santa Barbara Daily News for several years.

name: Michael Redmon
write_up: Michael Redmon: a librarian and author. As of 2016 Redmon was the Director of Research at the Santa Barbara Historical Museum and a contributor to the Santa Barbara Independent.

name: Milton Green Sublette
write_up: Milton Green Sublette (1800-36): mountain man and fur trader. During the 1830s he was among those who blazed trails through the northern Rocky Mountains, clearing the way for wagons to travel west on the Oregon Trail. George Nidever met Sublette and his trappers at Pierre’s Hole, Idaho.

name: Minnie Warren
write_up: Minnie Warren (1849-78): circus performer and signer who was born with proportionate dwarfism. She toured with P. T. Barnum.
notes: P. T. Barnum: The Legend and the Man by A. H. Saxon pg. 209-210; http://www.barnummuseumexhibitions.org/apps/blog/show/10206850-mourning-minnie

name: Miss Sinclair
write_up: Miss Sinclair: refers to Naomi Sinclair, the wife of Henry Nidever and sister-in-law of George Nidever. They had ten or eleven children.

name: Mon-a-nee
write_up: Mon-a-nee (later Won-a-nee): the otter Karana nurses back to health in Scott O'Dell's Island of the Blue Dolphins (1960).

name: Mr. J. B. Banning
write_up: Mr. J. B. Banning: founder of the Banning Wool Company in Avalon, California, in 1898.

name: Myra Berry Brown
write_up: Myra Berry Brown (1918-95): a literary agent, script analyst, reviewer, and author of children's books. Brown worked for Paramount Pictures and wrote for the Los Angeles Times.

notes: Robert Ernest Cowan, "Alexander S. Taylor, 1817-1876: First Bibliographer of California," California Historical Society Quarterly 12, 1 (1933): 18-24

name: Gorham Hayden Nye
write_up: Gorham Hayden Nye (1802-78): Massachusetts-born sailor who served on a number of roundtrip whaling voyages from New England before relocating to the California coast in 1831. His wife, Lydia Rider Drew, joined him in California. Nye engaged in the hide & tallow and otter skin trades, serving as master of various ships, including the Loriot, Bolivar Liberator, and Fama. Lum Jo, one of the first Chinese men in California, served as a cabin steward for Nye. He later became a successful landowner in Hawaii.
notes: Three page note sheet on Captain Gorham Hayden Nye compiled by Linda Bentz, Chinese Historical Society of Southern California (IBD funded research).

name: Stephen Simmonds
write_up: Stephen Simmonds (sometimes Steven Simmons): sea otter hunter. Along with Allen Light, Isaac Sparks, and others, he was one of the men who testified to the presence of unlicensed foreign ships hunting on the Channel Islands. In 1839, Governor Alvarado had ordered the mayor of Santa Barbara to make a special investigation into contraband. In 1843, Simmonds was among the otter hunting party of the Oajaca, which anchored near San Nicolas Island. This party (which also included George Nidever, Isaac Sparks, Job Dye, and Nicholas Dawson) saw evidence of the Lone Woman’s presence on the island.

notes:
Britannica Academic, s.v. "William Howard Taft," accessed March 11, 2017, http://academic.eb.com/levels/collegiate/article/William-Howard-Taft/70903.
"William Howard Taft." The White House. March 08, 2017. Accessed March 11, 2017. https://www.whitehouse.gov/1600/presidents/williamhowardtaft.

name: Nancy Nidever
write_up: Nancy Nidever (1811-50): sister of George Nidever (junior). She was born in Sullivan County, Tennessee, and died in Crawford County, Arkansas.

name: Narciso Durán
write_up: Narciso Durán (1776-1846): a Spanish-born Franciscan priest.  He resided at Mission Santa Barbara between 1833-46, following many years in residence at Mission San Jose.  During his almost 40 years as a missionary in California, Durán served in a number of administrative roles including president of the mission system, commissary prefect, vicar-general, and administrator of the diocese. Durán unsuccessfully resisted the secularization of the missions.
notes: Maynard Geiger, Franciscan Missionaries in Hispanic California, 1769-1848: A Biographical Dictionary

name: Nathan Winship
write_up: Nathan Winship: a sailor and captain of the ship Albatross out of Boston around the time the Nicoleño people were removed to mainland California in 1835.

name: Nathaniel B. Emerson
write_up: Nathaniel B. Emerson: a physician who published The Long Voyages of the Ancient Hawaiians and Unwritten Literature of Hawaii and who translated from the Hawaiian Hawaiian Antiquities: Moolelo Hawaii, by David Malo (1898).

name: Nicholas Dawson
write_up: Nicholas Dawson (1819-1903): an American settler who moved west to California, then Texas, in the mid nineteenth century. Raised in Glasgow, Pennsylvania, Dawson joined a large company travelling to California in 1841, when he was 19. Once there, Dawson engaged in a number of economic activities, including otter hunting. In his memoir California in '41, Texas in '51, Memoirs of N. Dawson, he states that he accompanied Isaac Sparks and George Nidever on a trip to San Nicolas Island where they found traces of the Lone Woman.
notes:
Dawson, Nicholas, California in '41, Texas in '51, Memoirs of N. Dawson. 1901. pp. 5-6,12, 59-60.

Catalog of Copyright Entries: Part 1, Books, Group 1, Volume 30. United States Government Printing Office, Washington, 1934, p. 1155.
https://books.google.com/books?id=cqIhAQAAIAAJ&pg=PA1155&lpg=PA1155&dq=nicholas+dawson+memoirs+california&source=bl&ots=KfvMNLW1-f&sig=q-N5BgunsDcVyQGQzcok6PLq25E&hl=en&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwj8zMqHtO3SAhXLLyYKHVQZCoQQ6AEIMjAF#v=onepage&q=nicholas%20dawson%20memoirs%20california&f=false

name: Nicholas Den
write_up: Nicholas Den (1812-62): rancher and owner of the Dos Pueblos ranch. After family misfortunes drove him and his younger brother Richard out of Ireland seeking to regain their fortunes in the United States, Den eventually arrived in Santa Barbara in 1836. He became a Mexican citizen and married Rosa Hill, the daughter of Daniel Hill, who had arrived in California in the 1820s and married into a Californio family (the Ortegas). As mission lands were being secularized following Mexican independence from Spain, Nicholas Den and his wife received a lease of lands formerly belonging to Mission Santa Barbara; in 1842, they purchased this land from the Mexican government. However, after California became a part of the United States, the legality of the sale was questioned, and a US court invalidated the contract. Leaving the ranch, Den became involved in Santa Barbara civic law until his death in 1862.
notes: Redmon, Michael. "Nicholas and Richard Den." Nicholas and Richard Den. March 11, 2010. Accessed February 25, 2017. http://www.independent.com/news/2010/mar/11/nicholas-and-richard-den/.

name: Nicholas of Myra
write_up: Nicholas of Myra (270?-345?): a Greek saint. Details of his life are uncertain, but tradition holds that he was persecuted and imprisoned under the Roman emperor Diocletian, released after Constantine took the throne, and may have been a member of the Council of Nicaea. He has been identified as the person upon whom the tradition of Santa Claus is based.

name: Nicolas
write_up: Nicolas (280-343 CE): patron saint of sailors and children. A Grecian bishop who served in present-day Turkey, St. Nicolas was known for his generosity. One legend popular in the Middle Ages claimed that he secretly left dowries in the home of three impoverished sisters, saving them from a life of prostitution. This story became the inspiration for Santa Claus. St. Nicolas died on December 6, and this day became St. Nicolas (or Feast) Day.
notes:
Britannica Academic, s.v. "St. Nicholas Day," accessed March 11, 2017, http://academic.eb.com/levels/collegiate/article/St-Nicholas-Day/389230
Handwerk, Brian. "St. Nicholas to Santa: The Surprising Origins of Mr. Claus." National Geographic. March 10, 2017. Accessed March 11, 2017. http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2013/12/131219-santa-claus-origin-history-christmas-facts-st-nicholas/.
"St. Nicholas Biography." Biography.com. December 07, 2016. Accessed March 11, 2017. http://www.biography.com/people/st-nicholas-204635#synopsis.

name: Nikolai Petrovich Rezanov
write_up: Nikolai Petrovich Rezanov (1764-1807): a Russian trader, explorer, and nobleman who was one of the first major shareholders of the Russian American Company. Seeking to facilitate trade between the Russian outpost in Sitka, Alaska, and Spanish settlements in California, Rezanov worked with the Spanish colonial governor Don José Argüello to develop a trade deal. While in California, Rezanov sought to marry Maria de la Concepcion Marcela Argüello, the Spanish comandante’s daughter, perhaps in part to bolster favor for the treaty. However, Rezanov died en route to St. Petersburg, before officials in Russia or Spain ever considered the deal. The love story between the Russian and Spaniard became a popular legend in California, as Maria de la Concepcion entered a convent and never married.
notes:
https://www.britannica.com/biography/Nikolay-Petrovich-Rezanov https://www.nps.gov/prsf/learn/historyculture/love-story.htm http://www.americanjourneys.org/aj-128/summary/
Owens, Kenneth N.. Samuel and Althea Stroum Bks. : Empire Maker : Aleksandr Baranov and Russian Colonial Expansion into Alaska and Northern California. Seattle, US: University of Washington Press, 2015, pp. 164-5, 212-6, Accessed July 20, 2017. ProQuest ebrary.
Black, Lydia, Russians in Alaska, 1732-1867. Fairbanks, University of Alaska Fairbanks, 2004, pp. 105-6, 176, Thomas Cooper Library, University of South Carolina, Columbia.

name: Ninetta Wiley Eames Payne
write_up: Ninetta Wiley Eames Payne (1852-1944): an author and editor. She was associated with Overland Monthly and Out West Magazine, was a member of the bohemian scene in Berkeley, California, in the early twentieth century, and was a feminist. She married for the third time at age 84.
notes:
http://www.findagrave.com/cgi-bin/fg.cgi?page=gr&GRid=78418230; Early Days and Writers of the Overland: Ninetta Eames--"The Thoreau of the Pacific Coast"By George Wharton James.
Overland Monthly and Out West Magazine (1868-1935) No. 4 (Aug 1923): 5.; http://heritageramblings.net/2014/04/21/mystery-monday-fred-foster-springer-and-ninetta-wiley-eames-payne/

[pages 147, 155, 164-6]

[see Ninetta Eames Payne on page 454--lots to review]

name: Nuño Beltrán de Guzmán
write_up: Nuño Beltrán de Guzmán (1490?-1558): a Spanish soldier, politician, and conquistador. Guzmán was governor of the Pánuco province in what is now Mexico and established the first audencia, or court, in what was then New Spain. His governorship was marked by violent treatment of the Native population and political abuses. He was eventually ousted as governor, imprisoned, and returned to Spain.
notes:
"Nuño de Guzmán." Columbia Electronic Encyclopedia, 6Th Edition (December 2013): 1. MasterFILE Premier, EBSCOhost (accessed July 7, 2014).

Encyclopedia of Latin America (2010)
Ida Altman
"Guzmán, Nuño de (Nuño Beltrán de Guzmán) (b. ca. 1490–d. 1558)"
page 158

name: Omset
write_up: Omset (1814?-?): a Chumash and Santa Rosa Island Native interviewed by Stephen Bowers. In the interview, Omset told Bowers that he was removed from his natal island around 1818 and taken to the Santa Barbara Mission. He may be Anacleto (or Aniceto) Pahilachet.

name: Unidentified man
write_up: One of our most respectable citizens: This is likely an error. Upon her arrival, María Aleja was sponsored for baptism by her godmother María Ignacia Amador.

name: Origen
write_up: Origen (185?-253?): a third-century Catholic theologian and scholar known for his exegetical analysis of Biblical texts. His theology assumed an allegorical interpretation of the Bible, a hierarchical understanding of the Trinity of the Christian God, and the ultimate restoration of all persons. His doctrines became quite controversial.

name: Oscar Loew
write_up: Oscar Loew: a chemist and mineralogist. Loew collected specimens on Santa Cruz Island in 1875 for the United States National Museum (the Smithsonian).

name: Osgood Church Wheeler
write_up: Osgood Church Wheeler (1816-91): a Baptist pastor and missionary who served in the first Protestant church erected in California, helped organize the San Francisco Baptist Association and the California Baptist Education Society, and edited the Pacific Banner. Wheeler later served as secretary of the California Agricultural Society and as a clerk of the California Assembly. During the Civil War, he organized numerous soldiers' aid societies. He worked for the Central Pacific Railroad Company in the later part of his life.
notes:
Selected Letters of Osgood Church Wheeler
Sandford Fleming and Osgood Church Wheeler
California Historical Society Quarterly, Vol. 27, No. 1 (Mar., 1948), pp. 9-18
Published by: University of California Press in association with the California Historical Society
Article DOI: 10.2307/25156075
Article Stable URL: http://www.jstor.org/stable/25156075

http://cdnc.ucr.edu/cgi-bin/cdnc?a=d&d=PRP18910425.2.42#

name: P. T. Jackson
write_up: P. T. Jackson: a sailor. Jackson owned the Boston ship Charon that was captained by Isaac Whittemore around the time the Nicoleño people were removed from San Nicolas Island to mainland California.

name: Pacifico Batista
write_up: Pacifico Batista: a man who was supposedly a resident of the Argentine village Itacuarare before becoming known as "Tarzan." The story goes that Pacifico Batista disappeared into the jungle around 1927, after arguing with a woman, and in 1943, reappeared "wild" and initially unable to speak. When he was able to communicate again, he identified himself as Pacifico Batista and expressed the desire to return to the jungle. News reports at the time referred to him as a "Tarzan."

name: Padre de la Ascencion
write_up: Padre de la Ascencion: a priest who may have accompanied Cabrillo on his exploration of the present-day western coastline of the United States from 1542-43.

name: Pamela Munro
write_up: Pamela Munro: a linguist (PhD, University of California, San Diego) and professor emerita at the University of California, Los Angeles. Her expertise is in California Native languages, and she has revised and compiled several language dictionaries. Her published works include Mojave Syntax (1976) and Chickasaw: An Analytical Dictionary (1994, with Catherine Willmond).

name: Pascal P. Pope
write_up: Pascal P. Pope: a merchant and businessman. Pope partnered with William H. Boardman from 1807-29 to run Boardman & Pope, a commerce and shipping venture out of Boston, Massachusetts. Pope continued to run the business on his own until 1837.

name: Patricia Campbell Hearst
write_up: Patricia Campbell Hearst (1954-): a public figure and the granddaughter of William Randolph Hearst, the nineteenth-century newspaper magnate. As a teenager, Patty Hearst was kidnapped by the Symbionese Liberation Army and photographed and videotaped participating in their illegal activities. After being rescued, she was tried and found guilty of crimes. The Hearst case became famous in part because the defense claimed she was a victim of Stockholm syndrome and had been brainwashed. After her incarceration, Hearst received a partial pardon from US President Jimmy Carter and a full pardon from US President Bill Clinton. Since this pardon, she has done some acting and made television appearances (as of 2016).
notes:
Women criminals : an encyclopedia of people and issues
volume 2 (2012)
Bemis, Michael
"Hearst, Patty (1954–)
pages 457-460

name: Patricia Sauers
write_up: Patricia Sauers: the Range Liaison Specialist for the NAVAIR Ranges, located at China Lake and Point Mugu, California as well as Patuxent River, Maryland. As of 2016, Sauers was a contributing writer for Navy publications providing capability awareness of the Ranges.
notes: [see Rachel Manuszak's email with Ms. Sauers 24-7 June 2014]

name: Paul Schumacher
write_up: Paul Schumacher: an archaeologist associated with the United States Coast Survey. Schumacher's work on the Channel Islands contributed to a greater understanding of Native work practices, including the construction of mortars and pestles found across the islands.

name: Persifer Frazer Smith
write_up: Persifer Frazer Smith (1798-1858): an officer in the United States Army, serving in both the Second Seminole and Mexican-American Wars. Smith was also appointed as one of California’s military governors before it entered the Union.

name: Peter C. Howorth
write_up: Peter C. Howorth (1944-): a marine wildlife specialist whose work focuses on Santa Barbara and the California Channel Islands. Howorth has been involved in several marine and environmental protection organizations, and has provided marine censusing information for the US Navy’s Marine Mammal Program and the National Oceanic Atmospheric Administration fisheries.

name: Peter the Wild Boy
write_up: Peter the Wild Boy (1713?-85): an abandoned youth who was discovered in Germany and, after being brought to the attention of England's King George I (who was also Elector of Hanover), he was taken to the palace as a kind of "human pet." Peter crawled on all fours, never learned to speak, and disliked wearing clothing. He was a source of interest among Enlightenment thinkers who were struggling to define what it meant to be human. He may have had what is now known as Pitt-Hopkins syndrome.

name: Phil Cummings Orr
write_up: Phil Cummings Orr (1903-91): an archaeologist who pioneer the use of radiocarbon dating on the California Channel Islands and furthered understanding of the cultural and environmental history of this region.  Orr served as Curator of Paleontology and Anthropology at the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History from 1938 to 1969.  During this period, he led many archaeological expeditions to the California Channel Islands, including several to San Nicolas Island in 1945.  Most importantly, he spent twenty-one field seasons on Santa Rosa Island, living in quite primitive conditions, and discovered in 1959 the oldest traces of human inhabitation yet found in North America, the 13,000-year-old remains of an individual now known as the “Arlington Springs Man.”  While Orr remains a controversial figure in the history of California archaeology, employing excavation tactics no longer in use today, his work was pivotal to later researchers
notes:
http://www.islapedia.com/index.php?title=ORR,_Phil_Cummings
https://www.sbnature.org/crc/325.html
http://www.teachchannelislands.org/tales/arlington-springs-man West of the West Clip, 2:30
Linda Bentz, Torben Rick, and Breanna Campbell, “Archaeology of an Archaeologist,” forthcoming (post 2017)

name: Philip III
write_up: Philip III (1578-1621): king of Spain from 1598 until 1621. Philip inherited the throne at a time of economic turmoil but European peace, and he relegated much of his kingdom’s governance to one of his ministers, Francisco Gómez de Sandoval y Rojas.

name: Philip Mills Jones
write_up: Philip Mills Jones (1870-1916): a physician and lawyer. Jones attended New York University and Long Island College Hospital before practicing medicine. He later moved across the country to work with the University of California Department of Archaeology and became very involved with the California Medical Association, serving as secretary and editor of the California State Journal of Medicine for a number of years. Jones was also a member of the Board of Trustees of the American Medical Association, National Committee of One Hundred on Public Health, and the American Academy of Medicine. His authored Archaeological Investigations on Santa Rosa Island in 1901 (1956).

name: Phineas T. Barnum
write_up: Phineas T. Barnum (1810-91): a showman responsible for the Barnum & Bailey Circus (founded 1881), known as "the greatest show on earth." The circus traveled across the United States until its last performance (with the Ringling Brothers) in 2017.

name: Phoebe Apperson Hearst
write_up: Phoebe Apperson Hearst (1842-1919): a philanthropist. Hearst, who inherited the fortune her husband made in mining, became a benefactor of the University of California and served on its board of regents from 1897 until her death, becoming the first woman to hold such a position. She developed a particular interest in anthropology and funded efforts to systematically collect materials for the university’s museum. Other causes she supported include education, public libraries, and women’s suffrage. She was the mother of newspaper giant William Randolph Hearst.

name: María del Pilar
write_up: María del Pilar (1807-60): a Ventureño Chumash woman who visited the Lone Woman in Santa Barbara in 1853. J. P. Harrington’s informant Luisa Ygnacio said that Pilar and Martina (a Cruzeño Chumash woman) traveled from Ventura to Santa Barbara to see the Lone Woman, collecting clams along the way as a gift. Pilar tried to speak to the Nicoleña in Fernandeño (a Gabrielino dialect) but was unsuccessful in communicating.

name: Pío de Jesus Pico
write_up: Pío de Jesus Pico (1801-94): the last governor of California under Mexican rule. Secularization and land redistribution was completed during his term in office, which ended with the Mexican-American War. No evidence has been found that he issued a request for the Nicoleños to be removed to the California mainland; as of 2016, the party responsible for sending the Peor es Nada to San Nicolas Island has not been determined.

name: Pliny Earle Goddard
write_up: Pliny Earle Goddard (1869-1928): an anthropologist who received his PhD from the University of California, Berkeley. Goddard served as a school principal in various locations and then as a missionary with the Woman's Indian Aid Association in California, observing the Hupa and inspiring his anthropological career. Goddard taught anthropology at the University of California, Berkeley, and Columbia University. He was also a curator of the American Museum of Natural History, edited the American Anthropologist, and founded and co-edited (with Franz Boas) the International Journal of American Linguistics.

name: Policarpio
write_up: Policarpio (1833-1856): a Chumash fisherman living in the Santa Barbara area who accompanied George Nidever to San Nicolas Island in 1853, when the Lone Woman returned with the crew to Santa Barbara. Policarpio's wife, Luisa Ygnacio, told J. P. Harrington that Policarpio brought home from San Nicolas Island two feather capes and a shell fishhook that belonged to the Lone Woman.
notes: Harrington notes research in progress (forthcoming publication by Susan Morris, John Johnson, and Steve Schwartz). Travis Hudson, “Recently Discovered Accounts Concerning the ‘Lone Woman’ of SNI,” Journal of CA and Great Basin Anthropology 3 (1981): 195-96; Thomas Blackburn, December’s Child: A Book of Chumash Oral Narratives Collected by JP Harrington (Berkeley: U of CA Press, 1975): 19.

name: Potter
write_up: Potter: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part.
notes: Weber, David J. The Taos Trappers: The Fur Trade in the Far Southwest, 1540-1846. Norman, Oklahoma: University of Oklahoma Press, 1971.

name: Powell Weaver
write_up: Powell Weaver: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part. He continued west to California.
notes: Weber, David J. The Taos Trappers: The Fur Trade in the Far Southwest, 1540-1846. Norman, Oklahoma: University of Oklahoma Press, 1971.

name: R. Garner Curran
write_up: R. Garner Curran: journalist, editor, and publisher in Southern California.

name: R. Scott Byram
write_up: R. Scott Byram: archaeologist (PhD, University of California, Berkeley) and author of Triangulating Archaeological Landscapes: The US Coast Survey in California, 1850-1895 (2013).

name: Ralph Waldo Emerson
write_up: Ralph Waldo Emerson (1803-82): an American essayist, lecturer, and poet as well as the central figure of the Transcendentalist movement. Some of his most famous works include "Nature" and "The American Scholar," and he inspired writers such as Henry David Thoreau and Walt Whitman.

name: Ramo
write_up: Ramo: Karana's younger brother in Scott O'Dell's Island of the Blue Dolphins. He is the reason that Karana dives overboard to return to the island, resulting in her eighteen years of solitude. Ramo is O'Dell's fictional invention.

name: Ramón Salazar
write_up: Ramón Salazar: a Mexican boy and the protagonist of Scott O'Dell's Newbery Honor book, The Black Pearl (1967).

name: Reading McCoy
write_up: Reading McCoy: an otter hunter who hunted with both Carl Dittman and George Nidever in the 1840s.

name: Reginald Dennis “Denny” Lamberth
write_up: Reginald Dennis “Denny” Lamberth (1933-1976): resident of San Nicolas Island as a child in the late 1930s. His parents, Reginald H. Lamberth and Evelyn Margaret, were managers of a sheep estate on the island.

name: Reginald H. Lamberth
write_up: Reginald H. Lamberth: the manager of San Nicolas Island’s sheep ranch during the 1939 Los Angeles County Museum exploration of the island; this exploration was headed by Arthur Woodward and Don Meadows.

name: Remus
write_up: Remus: one of the twins credited as the founders of Rome in Roman mythology.

name: René L. Vellanoweth
write_up: René L. Vellanoweth (1965-): coastal archaeologist with particular interest in maritime peoples. Vellanoweth completed his PhD in anthropology at the University of Oregon and has conducted research and published widely on San Nicolas Island for two decades. He and his students have been pivotal to the work of uncovering the cave used by the Lone Woman of San Nicolas, among others.

name: Samuel H. Willey
write_up: Reverend Dr. Samuel H. Willey (1821-1914): a Presbyterian minister and leader in education. Willey attended Dartmouth College and Union Theological Seminary before being sent to California by the American Home Missionary Society. Willey became a leader in the community, serving as chaplain of the state's Constitutional Convention and helping to found numerous institutions, including Monterey's first public library, what is today Mills College, the Hamlin School, and California College (which became the University of California, Berkeley).

name: Rhoda Kellogg
write_up: Rhoda Kellogg (1898-1987): a children's school administrator and author. Kellogg served as a preschool administrator for many years, during which time she collected millions of drawings by preschool-aged children worldwide. Based on analysis of these drawings, she claimed that the artwork of all children begins the same; over time, however, it comes to reflect the culture in which the artist is raised. Kellogg lectured internationally and her published works include What Children Scribble and Why (1959) and Analyzing Children's Art (1969).
notes:
http://www.worldcat.org/search?qt=worldcat_org_all&q=rhoda+kellogg

San Francisco Chronicle
August 29, 1987
page 16
Obituary

name: Richard Applegate
write_up: Richard Applegate: a linguist involved in the rejuvenation of the Samala Chumash language, which he researched for his PhD dissertation in linguistics from the University of California, Berkeley, in the 1960s. Using J. P. Harrington's ethnographic notes, Applegate constructed a dictionary and grammar that is currently being used by members of the Santa Ynez Band of Chumash to reclaim the language of their ancestors.

name: Richard Barnes Mason
write_up: Richard Barnes Mason (1797-1850): a soldier. Mason was an officer in the United States Army, serving in both the Black Hawk and Mexican-American War. He was also one of the military governors of California appointed prior to its entry into the Union.

name: Richard Henry Dana, Jr.
write_up: Richard Henry Dana, Jr. (1815-82): a lawyer and politician. Dana attended Harvard University and, despite his privileged background as the descendent of an important colonial family, enlisted as a merchant seaman, traveling to Alta California. He attended law school upon his return and became a maritime rights and anti-slavery activist, helping to establish the Free-Soil Party. Dana's published works include Two Years Before the Mast (1840) and The Seaman's Friend: A Treatise on Practical Seamanship (1841).

name: Richard King
write_up: Richard King: an illustrator, sailor, educator, and independent scholar. King earned a PhD at the University of St. Andrews; his research interests focus on the connection between sea voyage narratives and natural history. King has taught "Literature of the Sea" at The Williams College-Mystic Seaport Maritime Studies Program and authored a regular column, "Animals in Sea History," in Sea History magazine. He is the author of Lobster (2011) and The Devil's Cormorant: A Natural History (2013).

name: Rider/Horseman
write_up: Rider/Horseman: the identity of the rider is unknown. George Nidever's account states that one of his sons (most likely his oldest child, Mark/Marcos, who would have been eleven at the time) was waiting for them on horseback. Other sources identify the horseman or rider on the beach as Dr. Samuel L. Brinkerhoff or S. L. Shaw (likely Dr. James Barron Shaw).

name: Tadeu Amat y Brusi
write_up: Right Reverend (Bishop) Tadeu Amát y Brusi (1810-78): a Barcelona-born priest who became the first bishop of Los Angeles. He was sent to Louisiana as a missionary after his ordination, later served as a rector at a Philadelphia seminary, and then was appointed Bishop of Monterey, an area extending from San Jose to the Mexican border. As Los Angeles grew in importance, Amát y Brusi petitioned the Vatican to move the episcopal see, and he was appointed Bishop of Monterey-Los Angeles in 1873. He oversaw construction of Los Angeles' first cathedral. While some accounts suggest that he sent the Lone Woman's belongings to the Vatican, evidence has not yet been found confirming that the belongings were sent to, or reached, that destination.
notes:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Monterey_in_California [for list that couldn't be found elsewhere in such a straightforward format & birth/death years]

http://www.santabarbaraca.gov/services/community/historic/historysb/timeline.asp

https://archive.org/stream/cu31924028920077#page/n13/mode/2up/search/amat; http://famvin.org/wiki/Thaddeus_Amat_y_Brusi

name: Robert Bean
write_up: Robert Bean: captain of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part. He returned to Arkansas once the group reached New Mexico.
notes: Weber, David J. The Taos Trappers: The Fur Trade in the Far Southwest, 1540-1846. Norman, Oklahoma: University of Oklahoma Press, 1971.

name: Robert Field Stockton
write_up: Robert Field Stockton (1795-1866): Naval commodore most famous for securing California for the United States during the Mexican-American War.

name: Robert Fleiming Heizer
write_up: Robert Fleiming Heizer (1915-79): an anthropologist whose work centered on the prehistoric and historic peoples of western North America, and particularly California and Nevada (although he also contributed significantly to studies in Olmec archaeology). A professor at the University of California, Berkeley, Heizer founded and directed the University of California Archaeological Survey from 1948-60. With Albert B. Elsasser, he edited its 55th report, "Original Accounts of the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island," a compilation of archival and published accounts about the Lone Woman’s life and "rescue."

name: Robert Larkin Brooks
write_up: Robert Larkin Brooks (1890-1973): a rancher and San Miguel Island lessee from 1917-48. In 1924 he partnered with Edward Vail and extended his sheep grazing onto San Nicolas Island.

name: Robert M. Norris
write_up: Robert Matheson Norris (1921-2012): a geologist (PhD, Scripps Institute). Norris taught throughout his long career at the University of California, Santa Barbara. From 1970-75, he served as director of the Santa Cruz Field Station. His research interests included Quaternary geology, Quaternary shelf sedimentation, and geomorphology (including study of sand dunes and shoreline erosion).

name: Robinson Crusoe
write_up: Robinson Crusoe: titular character of Robinson Crusoe (1719), the wildly-popular adventure story loosely based on the historical cast-away, Alexander Selkirk, who was stranded on the Juan Fernandez Islands in the South Pacific in the early eighteenth century. Robinson Crusoe was written by Daniel Defoe.

name: Roland Burrage Dixon
write_up: Roland Burrage Dixon (1875-1934): an anthropologist (PhD, Harvard University) who was considered an authority on the ethnology of Native groups in California. He worked for numerous institutions, including the Peabody Museum and the American Museum of Natural History; he was also a faculty member at Harvard. Dixon's published works include The Racial History of Man (1923) and The Building of Cultures (1928).

name: Romulus
write_up: Romulus: one of the twins credited as the founders of Rome in Roman mythology.

name: Rontu-aru
write_up: Rontu-aru: the son of the dog Rontu in Scott O'Dell's Island of the Blue Dolphins (1960).

name: Rontu
write_up: Rontu: the wild dog that Karana domesticates in Scott O'Dell's Island of the Blue Dolphins (1960).

name: Rosario Andrea Curletti
write_up: Rosario Andrea Curletti (1913?-1986): a genealogist, book collector, anthropologist, and historian. Curletti specialized in the history of Santa Barbara and in Chumash culture.

name: Roy Agee
write_up: Roy Agee (1901-79): a sheep rancher on San Nicolas Island. Agee moved to San Nicolas in 1929 with his wife, Margaret, in order to help her parents, Lyma and Edna Elliott, run their ranch on the island. In 1933, Robert Brooks sold his interest in San Nicolas Island to Roy Agee, and although the United States Navy took control of the island not long after, they were allowed to continue ranching, through a revocable permit, until 1943. Roy died in 1979 in San Luis Obispo, California.
notes:
"AGEE, Roy Esco." Islapedia. Accessed February 27, 2017. http://islapedia.com/index.php?title=AGEE%2C_Roy_Esco.
"San Nicolas Island Ranching Families." Islapedia. Accessed February 27, 2017. http://islapedia.com/index.php?title=San_Nicolas_Island_Ranching_Families.
Daily, Marla. Images of America: The California Channel Islands. Charleston, SC: Arcadia Publishing, 2012.

name: Roy Esco Agee
write_up: Roy Esco Agee (1901-79): a sheep rancher on San Nicolas Island during the 1939 Los Angeles County Museum exploration of the island, which was headed by Arthur Woodward and Don Meadows. Agee and his in-laws, the Elliotts, first oversaw the sheep ranch for Robert Brooks and later owned those assets.

name: Roy L. Moodie
write_up: Roy L. Moodie (1880-1934): a geologist and palaeopathologist. Moodie earned a PhD in geology from the University of Chicago and then held various teaching positions in the United States before becoming the first (and only) palaeopathologist at the Wellcome Historical Medical Museum in London. His published works include Paleopathology: An Introduction to the Study of Ancient Evidences of Disease and The Antiquity of Disease (1923). He later returned to the United States and is known in California for his early work in the La Brea Tar Pits in Los Angeles.
notes:
Journal Of Medical Biography
2013
Waldron T,
Roy Lee Moodie (1880-1934) and the beginnings of palaeopathology.

http://dml.cmnh.org/1995Nov/msg00366.html

http://www.worldcat.org/search?q=au%3Aroy+l.+moodie&qt=advanced&dblist=638; diss abstracts

name: Rudolph Altrocchi
write_up: Rudolph Altrocchi (1882-1953): a professor of Italian language and literature who taught at many US universities, ultimately retiring as chair of the Italian Department at University of California, Berkeley.  He earned his PhD at Harvard.

name: Rudolph Valentino
write_up: Rudolph Valentino (1895-1926): a film actor. His appearances include Eyes of Youth (1919) and The Sheik (1921).

name: Rufus P. Jennings
write_up: Rufus P. Jennings: a San Francisco-based businessman and member of the San Francisco Chamber Commerce.

name: S. L. Shaw
write_up: S. L. Shaw: name of a Ventura city councilman during this time. The S. L. Shaw referred to here may be a mistake, with the intended name being Dr. James Barron Shaw, who was reported to be on the beach at the time of the Lone Woman's arrival. Other reports suggest that it was one of Nidever's sons, Mark (Marcos) who was on horseback.

name: Saint Barbara
write_up: Saint Barbara: a Catholic saint and martyr. Tradition holds that she lived during the fourth century and was martyred for the faith as punishment by her disbelieving father, Dioscorus. Her veneration can be traced back to the seventh century.

name: Saint Francis of Assisi
write_up: Saint Francis of Assisi (1181?-1226): a Catholic saint and founder of the Franciscan order that played a tremendous role in shaping what is now the American west.

name: Saint Peter
write_up: Saint Peter: a Catholic saint and the first Pope (32-67). Tradition holds he was with Christ during his ministry and was appointed by him as successor to the Church. He is also credited as the author of many of the letters of the Christian New Testament.

name: Samuel Alfred Barrett
write_up: Samuel Alfred Barrett (1879-1965): an anthropologist. Barrett studied under Alfred Kroeber and Franz Boas and earned the University of California's first PhD in anthropology. Barrett built his career at the Milwaukee Public Museum.

name: Samuel Curtis Bruce
write_up: Samuel Curtis Bruce (1825-?): investor who purchased San Miguel Island to graze sheep.  He later sold his title to to George Nidever and made a bid on Santa Rosa Island.

name: Samuel Johnson
write_up: Samuel Johnson (1709-84): an English author, journalist, critic, and lexicographer. The most important literary figure of his day, Johnson is perhaps best remembered for his A Dictionary of the English Language (1755), the most significant dictionary of English until the appearance of the Oxford English Dictionary in 1884

name: San Clemente Hermit
write_up: San Clemente Hermit: Likely a solitary rancher stationed on the island to care for the declining number of sheep grazing on the island.

name: Saunders
write_up: Saunders: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part. He is described as "an old man."
notes: Weber, David J. The Taos Trappers: The Fur Trade in the Far Southwest, 1540-1846. Norman, Oklahoma: University of Oklahoma Press, 1971.

name: Scott O’Dell
write_up: Scott O'Dell (1898-1989): a journalist and author. During his lifetime, O'Dell was respected as an amateur historian, journalist, and award-winning author. His published works include an informal history of California entitled Country of the Sun (1957) and the children's books Island of the Blue Dolphins (1960 and 1961 Newbery), Sing Down the Moon (1970 and 1971 Newbery Honor), and Sarah Bishop (1980).

name: Sebastián Vizcaíno
write_up: Sebástian Vizcaíno (1550?–1615): A Spanish merchant, entrepreneur, and explorer who lived most of his life in Mexico. In 1596 he sailed to the Gulf of California to fish for pearls and establish a settlement, without success. He led an expedition in 1602-03 to search for a harbor on the California coast that could be used by Spanish galleons returning to Mexico from the Philippines. During this journey, Vizcaíno mapped the coastline originally explored by Juan Cabrillo and renamed a number of geographic features including the Bay of Monterey, the Santa Barbara Channel, and Catalina Island. His faulty description of the Bay of Monterey as providing a sheltered port encouraged Spanish expansion into the northwest.

name: Shelly Davis-King
write_up: Shelly Davis-King: an archaeologist (MA, University of Arkansas, Fayetteville; studies at the University of Cambridge). As of 2016, she was principal associate of Davis-King & Associates, a heritage resource management firm that works with western Native American tribes in the United States.

name: Sigmund Freud
write_up: Sigmund Freud (1856-1939): an Austrian scientist, psychoanalyst, and thinker. He attended the University of Vienna, where he studied under the leading European neurologists of the day before going into practice for himself, treating "hysteria" and other conditions affecting the mind and behavior. Freud is known today as the father of psychoanalysis; he revolutionized scientific and cultural understanding of the mind and transformed ideas about language and human relationships.

name: Sigurd Bernhard Hustvedt
write_up: Sigurd Bernhard Hustvedt (1882-1954): folklorist who taught in the English department of Universities of Illinois, Minnesota, and finally the University of California, Los Angeles. The son of Norwegian immigrants, he trained for the Lutheran ministry before studying folklore first at the University of California, Berkeley and then at Harvard, where he completed his PhD and met California Folklore journal's co-editor Archer Taylor. Together, Hustvedt and Taylor are considered the founders of modern folklore scholarship in the United States. Husvedt was a specialist in ballad criticism and musicology and author of the now classic Ballad Books and Ballad Men (1930).
notes: A Folklorist's Progress: Reflections of a Scholar's Life; American Folklore Scholarship: A Dialogue of Dissent; Journal of American Folklore obit: Vol. 68, No. 268 (Apr. - Jun., 1955), p. 200

name: Francis Drake
write_up: Sir Francis Drake (1542-96): a seafarer and explorer who is famous for circumnavigating the globe on his 1577-80 voyage.

name: Spanish scholar
write_up: Spanish scholar: the account does not provide further information, but later writers engaged with Bowers' claim. For example, in 1923, Father Zephyrin Engelhardt claimed there was no such Spanish scholar, while both Arthur Woodward (in 1957) and Travis Hudson (in 1981) suggested that Bowers was referring not to a scholar but to the burial register entry for the Lone Woman. In 1980, California author Adelaide Doran noted that the translation of the document had not yet come to light, but would have been produced about the same time as Hubert Bancroft's assistant E. F. Murray took down George Nidever and Carl Dittman's life stories, including their encounters with the Lone Woman.

name: Spencer Fullerton Baird
write_up: Spencer Fullerton Baird (1823-89): a naturalist, biologist, and professor. Though Baird attended Dickinson College (Pennsylvania), where he later became chair of the Natural History department, he was largely self-taught, corresponding with Audubon and other leaders in the field. Baird served as the first curator of the Smithsonian Institution (with which he was associated for the rest of his life). He also pioneered conservation on America's eastern coastline when appointed to the US Fish Commission.

name: Stehman Forney
write_up: Stehman Forney (1846-1917): a US Coast and Geodetic Survey officer. An 1879 map by Forney noted the location of an "Indian Cave" on San Nicolas Island. Field notes for the map were used as a guide for locating the site, which had long been buried by sand, in the early twenty-fiirst century. Archaeologists believe this cave was used by the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island as well as her ancestors for thousands of year before.

name: Stephen DeMoss Bowers
write_up: Stephen DeMoss Bowers (1832?-1907): amateur geologist, archaeologist, Methodist minister, and publisher of the Ventura Free Press. He moved from the Midwest to Southern California for his wife’s health in the 1870s, during which time he devoted more energy to his hobby of field research, financed by the Smithsonian and US Department of the Interior. In 1875, Bowers became the first archaeologist to examine the remains of Native settlements on San Nicolas and Santa Rosa Islands. He removed great numbers of skulls, tools, and artifacts, which were deposited in museums and private collectors (the Smithsonian holds more than two thousand and Harvard University’s Peabody Museum holds more than eight hundred relics excavated by Bowers in California and the Midwest). Later archaeologists largely discredited Bowers’ field work, viewing it as having done more harm than good. Bowers' field notes, collected by John Peabody Harrington, were published in 1997 as Noontide Sun: The Field Journals of the Reverend Stephen Bowers, Pioneer California Archaeologist.

name: Stephen Simmonds
write_up: Stephen Simmonds (sometimes Steven Simmons): sea otter hunter. Along with Allen Light, Isaac Sparks, and others, he was one of the men who testified to the presence of unlicensed foreign ships hunting on the Channel Islands. In 1839, Governor Alvarado had ordered the mayor of Santa Barbara to make a special investigation into contraband. In 1843, Simmonds was among the otter hunting party of the Oajaca, which anchored near San Nicolas Island. This party (which also included George Nidever, Isaac Sparks, Job Dye, and Nicholas Dawson) saw evidence of the Lone Woman’s presence on the island.
notes: Adele Ogden, The California Sea Otter Trade; https://worldconnect.rootsweb.ancestry.com/cgi-bin/igm.cgi?op=GET&db=tcbjammon&id=P633

name: Steven J. Schwartz
write_up: Steven J. Schwartz: archaeologist who spent his career working for the federal government, first with the US Army Corps of Engineers (1980-89) and then with the Naval Air Systems Command (1989-2013). Stationed at Point Mugu, California, he was for many years the Senior Archaeologist for San Nicolas Island. After searching for the cave inhabited by the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island for twenty years, he found it in 2012, shortly before his retirement. Schwartz completed his BA in archaeology at California State University, Long Beach in 1981.

name: Stuart Luther Peck
write_up: Stuart Luther Peck (1893-1959): an archaeologist and anthropologist. Peck attended Pomona College, Harvard University, and the University of California (Berkeley and Los Angeles). He was a survey archaeologist with the Southwest Museum (Los Angeles) and a museum technician with UCLA; he served as the first curator of the San Bernardino County Museum.

name: Susan Tibbles
write_up: Susan Tibbles: an assemblage artist and illustrator. Tibbles is the owner of her company, Susan Tibbles Illustrations, and a partner in The Project-Fine Art Zone Gallery in Santa Barbara, California. She is well-known for her largely political assemblage artwork for the Los Angeles Times’s op-ed section.

name: T. Wallace More
write_up: T. Wallace More: one of the very successful More brothers, all of whom became prominent figures of the development of the region around Santa Barbara in the mid-nineteenth century. Between 1859 and 1870, three of the brothers (A. P., T. Wallace, and Henry H. More) became sole proprietors of Santa Rosa Island and developed the island into a thriving sheep-raising venture.

name: Tainor
write_up: Tainor: a bird tamed by Karana in Scott O'Dell's Island of the Blue Dolphins (1960).

name: Tarzan
write_up: Tarzan: fictional protagonist of Tarzan of the Apes, written by Edgar Rice Burroughs and first published in the American magazine All Story in 1912 (reprinted as a novel in 1914, it became the first of a long series of Tarzan books). Tarzan is a feral child born in the African jungle and raised by apes.

name: Ted Sell
write_up: Ted Sell (1920-89): a journalist. Sell attended Drake University (Iowa) and was a combat correspondent with the Marine Corps before spending two decades with the Los Angeles Times. Sell later edited the Sacramento Bee, was an assistant to the editor and chairman of McClatchy Newspapers, and founded and edited the McClatchy News Service, which served newspapers in six states.

name: Bearded Lady
write_up: The Bearded Lady (1860-1902): stage name of Annie Jones, a circus performer whose hirsutism caused excessive facial hair, even at a young age. She toured with P. T. Barnum and was featured in his "freak" shows.

name: The Cook
write_up: The Cook: one of the Native men who sailed with George Nidever’s sea otter hunting crew to San Nicolas Island in 1853. He stayed in camp while other Native men, George Nidever, Carl Dittman, and Colorado looked for the Lone Woman on San Nicolas Island. The cook's name was not recorded and his yet to be identified.

name: Lone Woman's Child
write_up: The Lone Woman’s Child: multiple accounts state that a young child or children of the Lone Woman failed to board the Peor es Nada in 1835. The Lone Woman may have had several children, but according to the narratives anthropologist J.P. Harrington collected from Native informants more than half a century after the event, she returned to San Nicolas Island when it became apparent that one child, a son, was still on the island. The story told to Harrington was that this son perished, likely while hunting at sea; the Lone Woman spent the remainder of her eighteen years on the island alone. Although Scott O’Dell did not consult J. P. Harrington’s ethnographic notes, his creation of a fictional younger brother (Ramo) for the Lone Woman (Karana) fits nicely with this evidence: the boy left behind was likely in middle childhood.

name: Mermaid
write_up: The Mermaid: a curiosity exhibited at P. T. Barnum's museum in New York and sent on traveling exhibits. Composed of a dried monkey head fused with a fish's tail.

name: Nova Scotian Giantess
write_up: The Nova Scotian Giantess (1846-88): stage name of Anna Swan, a circus performer of unusual height who gained fame in P. T. Barnum's "freak" shows.

name: Stout lady
write_up: The Stout Lady (?-1899): likely Elizabeth Charlotte Stice, who toured with P. T. Barnum’s Circus as "the Fat Lady."

name: Theodora Kroeber Quinn
write_up: Theodora Kroeber Quinn (1897-1979): an American anthropological writer who focused on California Indians. Quinn is most famous for her book Ishi in Two Worlds (1961). She was married to the famed anthropologist Alfred Kroeber.

name: Theodore Henry Hittell
write_up: Theodore Henry Hittell (1830-1917): a lawyer, editor of the San Francisco Bulletin, and author. After having been educated at numerous western colleges and at Yale, Hittell moved west to California in 1855. In California he was active in law, politics, and science. He wrote on legal issues, published History of California (1885; 1897), and presented numerous scientific papers to the California Academy of Sciences.

name: Thomas Blackburn
write_up: Thomas Blackburn: anthropologist (PhD, University of California, Los Angeles) who researched and published on the Chumash. Blackburn was a professor of geography and anthropology at California State Polytechnic University, Ponoma for many years. He frequently collaborated with Dee Travis Hudson.

name: Thomas F. Meagher
write_up: Thomas F. Meagher (1823-67): acting governor of the Montana Territory from 1865-66. He served as a United States Army Brigadier General for the Union during the American Civil War.

name: Thomas Jefferson Henley
write_up: Thomas Jefferson Henley (1808-78): a lawyer and politician. Henley attended Indiana University at Bloomington, was a representative of the Indiana State and Congressional Houses, served as the Superintendent of Indian Affairs for California, and was postmaster of San Francisco.

name: Thomas Jeffries
write_up: Thomas Jeffries (1809?-91): an English sailor who lived in Santa Barbara, California, in the mid-nineteenth century. According to an 1880 Scribner’s Monthly article, Father González Rubio paid Jeffries $200 to look for the Lone Woman on San Nicolas Island. Jeffries sailed to the island with George Nidever and several Native Calfiornia men in 1852. Although they found footprints and brush shelters, they did not find the Lone Woman.

name: Thomas Morland Hocken
write_up: Thomas Morland Hocken (1836-1910): an English physician, historian, and collector. Hocken attended Newcastle Upon Tyne College and the Ledwich School, establishing his career through various medical positions in Dunedin, Australia. He later became known as an historian of New Zealand and Pacific Native populations and for his sizeable collection of literature relating to the region, which was eventually given to the University of Otago.

name: Thomas Oliver Larkin
write_up: Thomas Oliver Larkin (1802-58): a merchant. Born in Massachusetts, Larkin arrived in California in 1832, opened a successful store in Monterey, and engaged in the Pacific trade. From 1844-48, a period marked by the rapid influx of US settlers following the Gold Rush, he served as the US Consul to Alta California.
notes: "George P. Hammond and the Larkin Papers," California Historical Society Quarterly; http://www.mchsmuseum.com/larkin_thomaso.html

name: Thomas Thompson
write_up: Thomas Thompson: with Albert West, co-owner of the Oakland-based Thompson & West publishing business.

name: Tom Barton
write_up: Tom Barton: fictional protagonist of Scott O'Dell's novel The Hawk That Dare Not Hunt by Day, a book set in sixteenth-century England.

name: Tom Hammonds
write_up: Tom Hammonds: a member of the 1830-33 hunting and trapping expedition of which George Nidever was a part.
notes: Weber, David J. The Taos Trappers: The Fur Trade in the Far Southwest, 1540-1846. Norman, Oklahoma: University of Oklahoma Press, 1971.

name: Tom Hanks
write_up: Tom Hanks (1956-): an actor and producer. His appearances include Big (1988), Forrest Gump (1994), and Cast Away (2000).

name: Trask
write_up: Trask: probably John Boardman Trask, one of the founding members of the California Academy of Sciences.

name: Tutok
write_up: Tutok: a character in Scott O'Dell's Island of the Blue Dolphins (1960). An Aleut girl brought to the island by sea otter hunters, she becomes Karana's first human friend after years of solitude.

name: Two-Head Nightingale
write_up: Two-Head Nightingale (1851-1912): stage name of Millie and Christine McCoy, conjoined twins born to a slave mother in North Carolina. Sold and exhibited locally, they eventually came under the control of a wealthy merchant who educated them in England. The women returned to the United States and performed as members of P. T. Barnum’s circus for many years.

name: Tzimtzincha-Tangaxuan II
write_up: Tzimtzincha-Tangaxuan II: king of the Purépecha (or Tarascan) people. He accepted peace terms from Hernán Cortés and converted to Christianity, but was later tortured and hanged by conquistador Nuño Beltrán de Guzmán.

name: Ulape
write_up: Ulape: Karana's older sister in Scott O'Dell's Island of the Blue Dolphins (1960).

name: Unidentified elderly Indian woman
write_up: Unidentified Indian woman: one of many Native peoples who visited the Nidever home in an attempt to communicate with the Lone Woman. Evidence suggests that none of them, including the "old woman," spoke the Lone Woman's language fluently, although this elderly woman and a man, Nolverto/Nolberto might have been able to communicate with her unevenly. At the time of the Lone Woman's arrival in Santa Barbara, at least one Nicoleño man removed from San Nicolas Island on the Peor es Nada was alive, but he and the Lone Woman never came into contact.
notes: Hudson1981 on Nolv[b]erto, otherwise documents cited themselves

name: Unidentified Indians
write_up: Unidentified Indians: men, likely associated with Mission Santa Barbara, who have not been identified at this time.

name: Unidentified Los Angeles resident
write_up: Unidentified Los Angeles resident: most documents refer to a Los Angeles resident "marrying" an unidentified Nicoleño woman. Neither Church nor mission documents between 1835-60 record a marriage between any prominent Los Angeles citizens and San Nicolas or other Channel Island Natives. It was common, however, for Indians to be taken as servants during this time.

name: Unidentified Los Angeles resident
write_up: Unidentified Los Angeles resident: most documents refer to a Los Angeles resident "marrying" an unidentified Nicoleño woman. Neither Church nor mission documents between 1835-60 record a marriage between any prominent Los Angeles citizens and San Nicolas or other Channel Island Natives. It was common, however, for Indians to be taken as servants during this time.

name: Unidentified Nicoleño woman
write_up: Unidentified Nicoleño woman: most documents refer to an unidentified Nicoleño woman as "married to" a Los Angeles resident. Neither Church nor mission documents between 1835-60 record a marriage between any prominent Los Angeles citizens and San Nicolas or other Channel Island Natives. It was common, however, for Indians to be taken as servants during this time.

name: Unidentified Nicoleño woman
write_up: Unidentified Nicoleño woman: most documents refer to an unidentified Nicoleño woman as "married to" a Los Angeles resident. Neither Church nor mission documents between 1835-60 record a marriage between any prominent Los Angeles citizens and San Nicolas or other Channel Island Natives. It was common, however, for Indians to be taken as servants during this time.

name: Unidentified Nicoleño woman
write_up: Unidentified Nicoleño woman: there is no evidence that a Nicoleña was removed from San Nicolas Island between the 1814 confrontation with Alaska Natives and the 1835 group removal of islanders on the Peor es Nada. It is likely that in the 1840s, Williams had an indigenous female from the mainland living in his household (he later had children with two Luiseña mistresses). However, George Nidever states in his memoir The Life and Adventures of George Nidever that Isaac Williams had a Nicoleña living with him after the 1835 removal.
notes: 1844 census. Susan's research (email 6/29 and 7/1, with census attachment) in probate records. Nidever memoir

name: Unidentified priest
write_up: Unidentified priest: possibly refers to Father Antonio de la Ascensión, one of the three Carmelite priests on Vizcaíno's expedition along the California coast. His account of the voyage was widely read.

name: Unidentified woman
write_up: Unidentified woman: possibly refers to items C. J. W. Russell deposited in the California Academy of Sciences collection in 1857, but their records do not list any needles.

name: Vasco Núñez de Balboa
write_up: Vasco Núñez de Balboa (1475-1519): a Spanish explorer and conquistador who served as the interim governor and captain general of the first stable Spanish settlement in South America, Santa María de la Antigua. In 1513, he became the first European to spot the Pacific Ocean from the American continent.

name: Victor Chauvin
write_up: Victor Chauvin (1844-1913): Belgian scholar of Semitic languages and the law. Chauvin taught Hebrew, Arabic, and Islamic Studies at the University of Liege and was also an important bibliographer. He is best known for his book Bibliographie des ouvrages arabes ou relatifs aux Arabes publiés dans l'Europe chrétienne de 1810 à 1885 (Bibliography of Arab works published in Christian Europe, 1810-1885). Chauvin was also an early folklorist.

name: Victor Herbert
write_up: Victor Herbert (1859-1924): a composer of operettas, operas, and light music most famous for operettas such as Babes in Toyland and The Fortune Teller. He also wrote the opera Natoma, which focuses on the romance between a Santa Barbara Indian and a naval captain.

name: Virgin Mary
write_up: Virgin Mary: the mother of Jesus and a character of veneration since the earliest days of Christianity. Also known as the Guadalupe Virgin, she is a common subject in Western art, music, and literature. In Roman Catholicism, Mary appeared in a vision to Juan Diego in 1531; he then created an extremely popular image of her surrounded by roses that has become a national symbol of Mexico and is still an object of devotion for Catholics in the twenty-first century.

name: Virginia Thomson
write_up: Virginia Thomson: no further information has been found excepting her association with the California Historical Society during this time.

name: Walter Edmund Roth
write_up: Walter Edmund Roth (1861-1933): an English physician and anthropologist. He was educated at the University College School in London and Oxford University. Roth moved to Australia where he documented aboriginal culture and was appointed the first Northern Protector of Aboriginals. He campaigned against abuses and exploitation of Native populations and contributed to legislation concerning the treatment of aboriginal populations. He is widely published.

name: Walter John de la Mare
write_up: Walter John de la Mare (1873-1956): an English poet and author. De la Mare's first career was as an accountant for the Standard Oil Company, and he wrote only in his spare time on scrap office paper. His career as a poet and author began after he was thirty, and his published works include Songs of Childhood (1902, as Walter Ramal), The Return (1910), and Peacock Pie (1913).

name: Walter Lindley
write_up: Walter Lindley (1852-1922): a physician and philanthropist. Lindley attended Long Island College Hospital before moving to Los Angeles and becoming a prominent community figure. There he served as a Health Officer and practiced medicine until his death. Lindley was instrumental in a number of efforts to improve public health and the conditions of orphans, helping establish many organizations to support these causes, including a free clinic, a reform school, an orphanage, the University of Southern California's College of Medicine, and one of the west coast's most successful hospitals (the California Hospital Medical Center, still in operation today).

name: Walter Scott Chaffee
write_up: Walter Scott Chaffee (1835?-94): founding resident of San Buenaventura, California. He was co-owner of the Chaffee and McKeeby General Store. In 1866, Chaffee was elected to the town’s first board of trustees.
notes: J. Winchester, Statues of California (1866); http://www.cityofventura.net/files/file/comm-develop/Downtown/Survey%20Report%20FULL.pdf; gravestone via ancestry.com

name: Warren K. Moorehead
write_up: Warren K. Moorehead: an amateur archaeologist, born in Italy and raised in Xenia, Ohio, who served as the first curator of archaeology for the Ohio Historical Society. As a young man, his collections of Ohio artifacts caught the attention of leading archaeologists, who hired him to excavate prehistoric sites associated with the Hopewell people. Moorehead is particularly well-known for his work at Fort Ancient and the Hopewell Mound Group; artifacts obtained were sent to the Columbian Exposition of 1893. As a member of the board of commissioners for the Bureau of Indian Affairs (appointed in 1909), Moorehead also advocated for living Native American peoples and worked to expose the abuses of Indian agents. Moorehead published widely, for collectors as well as professionals. His books include The Stone Age in North America (1910).

name: William Bean
write_up: William Bean: the son of Col. Robert Bean. He assisted his father in leading a forty-eight man expedition from Fort Smith to New Mexico. Although his father was not popular with the other men, William was well regarded.

name: Julien Foxen
write_up: William Benjamin Foxen (1796-1874): English merchant seaman who traded in Alta and Baja California before marrying into the Californio elite and acquiring a ranch; in Mexican California, he went by the name Guillermo Domingo, though local Indians called him "Don Julien." During the Mexican-American War, Foxen’s sympathies were with the United States and a legend arose that he tipped off John C. Frémont about an impeding ambush in Santa Barbara. During the American period, Foxen served as a horse-breeder and his adobe home became a stop on the stagecoach route.

name: William C. Parker
write_up: William C. Parker: aid to Jonathan Drake Stevenson during the Civil War. Parker later joined Stevenson in real estate ventures and cofounded Stevenson, Parker & Company.

name: William Dane Phelps
write_up: William Dane Phelps (1802-75): Boston-based sea captain and master of the ship Alert. Phelps traveled and traded along the California coast from 1840–42. He kept a journal and recorded details of the removal of the Nicoleños from San Nicolas Island, which he had learned from another sea captain, Thomas Robbins. In 1841, Phelps described meeting Black Hawk, who Phelps said came from San Nicolas Island, in San Pedro.

name: William DeWitt Alexander
write_up: William DeWitt Alexander (1833-1923): an educator, surveyor, linguist, and commissioner of the Kingdom of Hawaii during its transition to American statehood.

name: William Doyle
write_up: William Doyle: author of Man Alone (1953), with Scott O'Dell. He was incarcerated at Folsom Prison near Sacramento, California.

name: William E. Greenwell
write_up: William E. Greenwell (1824-86): a United States Coast survey agent. Greenwell worked on surveys of the Mississippi Gulf coast, the California Channel Islands, and along the Rio Grande River.

name: William "Bill" Fife
write_up: William Fife: otter hunter who partnered with both Carl Dittman and George Nidever in the 1840s.

name: William George Washington Harford
write_up: William George Washington Harford (1825-1911): a botanist. Harford served as the director of the California Academy of Sciences from 1876-86.

name: William Goodwin Dana
write_up: William Goodwin Dana (1797-1858): Boston sea merchant active in the Pacific trade between Hawaii and California. He settled in Santa Barbara, where he operated a store and married into the Californio elite (his wife was a daughter of Carlos Carrillo, provincial governor of Alta California, 1837-38). As a naturalized Mexican citizen he received a land grant and entered local politics serving, at various times, as captain of the port of Santa Barbara, mayor of Santa Barbara, and a judge. He supported California’s annexation by the United States and provided succor to John C. Frémont’s troops. George Nidever's association with Dana, a Mexican citizen, enabled him to hunt otter (using his license) after Mexico's 1830 prohibition against otter hunting by foreigners. In some sources, Dana is called "Captain Denny."

name: W. H. Burnham
write_up: William H. Burnham (1851-1934): a Connecticut man who moved to California in 1893 and subsequently became involved in the citrus and banking industries.
