name: William H. McDougall
write_up: William H. McDougall: a writer. In addition to his association with Overland Monthly during this time, he is the author of The Classical Elocutionist (1879), a primer in elocution.

name: William Healey Dall
write_up: William Healey Dall (1845–1927): a naturalist and explorer. Dall served on the Alaskan Western Union Telegraph Expedition, was appointed to the United States Coast and Geological Surveys, and served as an Honorary Curator at the Smithsonian Institution. Dall's published works include The Yukon Territory (1898).

name: William Heath Davis
write_up: William Heath Davis: a sailor and captain of the ship Isabella (owned by the Boston shipping firm Boardman & Pope) around the time the Nicoleño people were removed from San Nicolas Island to mainland California.

name: William Henry Ellison
write_up: William Henry Ellison (1878-1965): a historian of California and the Indians of the American Southwest (PhD, University of California, Berkeley). He edited The Life and Adventures of George Nidever: The Life Story of a Remarkable California Pioneer Told in his Own Words, and None Wasted (1937), the oral history recorded by E. F. Murray in 1878. Ellison was among the founding editors of the Pacific Historical Review and he served as president and director of the Santa Barbara Historical Society.

name: William Henry Holmes
write_up: William Henry Holmes (1846-1933): an archaeologist. Holmes was an artist with the US Geological Surveys, a curator with both the Field Museum of Natural History and the National Museum, and chief of the Bureau of American Ethnology. He travelled to archaeological sites throughout Mexico and Central America and was a member of the National Academy of Science. His published works include Art in Shell of the Ancient Americans (1883), and the Handbook of Aboriginal American Antiquities (1919).

name: William Howland
write_up: William Howland: may refer to one of three men. William Roberto Garcia Howland (1820-1888) ranched on Santa Catalina Island.  He fathered several sons, including his namesake William Percival Howland (1865-1892), about whom little is known, and Joseph Garcia Howland (1862-1923), who ranched on San Nicolas and Santa Catalina Islands.

name: William Lewis Sublette
write_up: William Lewis Sublette (1798-1845) the eldest of five brothers in a family of mountain men and fur traders. During the 1830s he was among those that blazed trails through the northern Rocky Mountains, clearing the way for wagons to travel west on the Oregon Trail. George Nidever meets Sublette and his trappers at Pierre’s Hole, Idaho.

name: William Milligan Sloane
write_up: William Milligan Sloane (1906-74): an author, publisher, and editor. Sloane worked for the Henry Holt and Company publishing house before establishing his own, William Sloane Associates. He also worked in academic publishing, serving as president of the Association of American University Presses and directing Rutgers University Press. During World War II he was a member of the Council on Books in Wartime. His published works include To Walk the Night (1937) and The Edge of Running Water (1939).

name: William O. Ayres
write_up: William O. Ayres: first Curator of Ichthyology at the California Academy of Science. He published descriptions of California's fish life in the San Francisco Pacific and in the Placer Times and Transcript (San Francisco).

name: William Shakespeare
write_up: William Shakespeare (1564-1616): famed English playwright and poet. Few records of his early life remain, but he gained recognition as an actor and poet in London before writing numerous plays greatly admired in his own lifetime and revered today (e.g., A Midsummer Night's Dream, Richard III, Hamlet).

name: William Sherley "Old Bill" Williams
write_up: William Sherley "Old Bill" Williams (1787?-1849): a travelling Methodist preacher, trapper, and frontiersman in the American West. Williams was part of the Joseph Brown party that surveyed the Santa Fe Trail and the Joseph Walker expedition to California. He also served as a guide for John Charles Frémont. Williams' distinct speech patterns, as captured by George F. Ruxton's novel Life in the Far West (1849), came to be a fictional standard for trappers.
notes:
"William Sherley Williams." Dictionary of American Biography. New York: Charles Scribner's Sons, 1936. Biography in Context. Web. 7 July 2014.

name: William T. Brigham
write_up: William T. Brigham: no further information has been found excepting his association with the American Anthropological Association during this time and his position as director and then curator of the Bernice Pauahi Bishop Museum in Honolulu, Hawaii.

name: William Tyndale
write_up: William Tyndale (c. 1494-1536): an English priest and scholar. Tyndale, who was educated at Oxford and Cambridge, was an outspoken advocate for reforming the Roman Catholic Church. He completed the first translation of the New Testament from Latin to English in 1525. Branded as a heretic, he remained in hiding while translating the Hebrew Bible. The English Reformation, following King Henry VIII's break from the Roman Catholic Church, enabled Tyndale to operate with comparative openness, but he was nevertheless captured by the Church's forces, convicted as a traitor and heretic, and burned at the stake. His work has influenced Biblical translation and linguistic scholarship from the King James Bible to the present day.

name: William Wadsworth
write_up: William Wadsworth: an American sea captain hired by the Russian American Company (RAC) to sail the Russian ship Il’mena. He took the RAC sea otter hunting groups to San Nicolas and San Clemente Islands in 1814. After depositing the hunters, Wadsworth continued to the Spanish California coast to trade for supplies. He sailed back to the Channel Islands to pick up the hunting groups.

name: William Waldo Drake
write_up: William Waldo Drake (1897-1977): journalist for the Los Angeles Times. After World War II, he toured Asia as the press representative for the Allied reparations commission.

name: William Workman
write_up: William Workman (1799-1876): English fur trapper who immigrated to the US in 1822 to join his older brother in Missouri. In 1825 he traveled west to New Mexico, where he formed a business partnership with John Rowland, engaging in fur trapping and operating a distillery and store near Taos. Workman was baptized, married a California woman, and became a naturalized citizen. His loyalties to Mexico were questioned, however, when he was identified with Texans interested in annexing New Mexico. Workman and Rowland fled to Los Angeles in 1841.

name: Woolly Horse
write_up: Woolly Horse: an exhibit of P. T. Barnum. Barnum claimed this horse was found by John Charles Frémont during his expedition through the Rocky Mountains.

name: Yda Hillis Addis
write_up: Yda Hillis Addis (1857-?): a journalist and author. She wrote widely for both English- and Spanish-language publications and was known for her strong female heroes and depictions of life in New Mexico. She became infamous for a tempestuous romantic life, with multiple personal relationships landing her in the court system. She disappeared from the public eye in disgrace and poverty.

name: Zachory Taylor
write_up: Zachary Taylor (1784-1850): the twelfth president of the United States. He rose to prominence as a general and war hero in both the War of 1812 and the Mexican-American War. Taylor was elected on the ticket of the Whig Party, but died after only sixteen months in office.

name: Zelia Maria Magdalena Nuttall
write_up: Zelia Maria Magdalena Nuttall (1857-1933): a self-taught archaeologist best-known for her work on pre-Columbian culture and for her skill in archival research. Nuttall, who was born in San Francisco, attended Bedford College (England) and lived and worked in Mexico for many years. She served as an honorary assistant in Mexican archaeology at the Peabody Museum at Harvard for more than four decades. She was a member of the American Association for the Advancement of Science and the American Folklore Society. In 1901, Franz Boas requested her assistance in gaining Phoebe Hearst's support to fund the establishment of an anthropology department at the University of California; they were successful. Nuttall's published works include The Fundamental Principles of Old and New World Civilizations(1901) and The Book of the Life of the Ancient Mexicans (1903).

name: Zenas Leonard
write_up: Zenas Leonard: a member of Benjamin Bonneville's 1833 party (under Joseph Reddeford Walker) to explore the Rocky Mountains.

name: Zephyrin Engelhardt
write_up: Zephyrin Engelhardt (1851-1934): a Franciscan historian. Born Charles Anthony Engelhardt in Germany, Father Zephyrin spent most of his life as a mission priest in California. His histories of the Spanish missions and the Franciscan presence in the American West remain standard references books.

name: Zeus
write_up: Zeus: the head of the Greek pantheon of gods and son of the Titan Cronus.

name: Zia
write_up: Zia: a supporting character in Scott O'Dell's Newbery Honor-winning novel The King's Fifth (1966 and 1967 Newbery Honor), which narrates Coronado's 16th-century exploration of what is now the United States' Southwest. Zia is described as being an Indian guide and interpreter.

name: Norberto
write_up: Norberto (1818-55): a Gabrielino shoemaker and saddlemaker from the Mission San Fernando area who lived in Santa Barbara in 1853.  According to Fernando Librado (Kitsepawit), a J. P. Harrington informant, Norberto, a Fernandeño speaker, could communicate with the Lone Woman. It is possible that Norberto is the one still unidentified native man to have accompanied George Nidever on the sea otter hunting trip to San Nicolas Island that ended with the Lone Woman returning with the crew to Santa Barbara.
